scope: Greater Cleveland / Cuyahoga County (CMHA). Landlord- and owner-facing.
rule: Writers may cite ONLY these facts for external numbers, dates, program rules and agency behavior. Anything not listed here is in `gaps` and must be OMITTED, never estimated. Our own service terms (placement = one month's rent, $100 inspection, 5% management) come from the owner and do not need a source.

facts.claim: CMHA has no landlord application or pre-approval process: its Leasing with CMHA page states, "Please note that CMHA does not have a pre-approval process for landlords or units," and the Landlord FAQ answers "How do I qualify as an HCVP Landlord?" with "No application process is required."

CMHA's HCV Administrative Plan confirms this at policy level: "The PHA does not formally approve an owner to participate in the HCV program," though it may deny a specific tenancy based on past owner behavior, conflict of interest, or other owner-related issues (24 CFR 982.306(e)).
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan (effective January 1, 2025), Section 9-I.C

facts.claim: CMHA directs owners to list vacant units free of charge: "CMHA has partnered with affordablehousing.com or OhioHousingLocator.org to provide free listing options for owners and landlords."
If an owner advertises elsewhere, CMHA asks that the ad say "vouchers are welcome" — CMHA does not market units on an owner's behalf.

CMHA publishes a seven-step path to becoming an HCVP landlord: (1) advertise the property, (2) attend Mandatory Landlord Orientation, (3) screen prospective tenants, (4) submit the RFTA packet, (5) accept the rent offer, (6) pass the inspection, (7) sign the HAP Contract and lease.

Tenant screening remains entirely the landlord's job — "It is the landlord's responsibility to screen prospective HCV tenants just as you would any other tenant" — and CMHA will provide the family's current address and, if known, the name and address of the landlord at the family's current and prior address.

CMHA runs an online intake form for prospective landlord partners at cmha.net/housing/landlords/online_inquiries.php ("To learn more about becoming a CMHA Landlord Partner, please share your information here").

CMHA's landlord Forms & Documents library holds 16 documents including the Automatic Deposit Form, Agent/Payee Form, Change of Ownership Form, Rent Adjustment Request Form, Landlord Portal Guide, Landlord Participation Guide, Inspection Checklist and the 2026 HAP Payment Calendar.
CMHA is running a recurring Landlord Working Group with sessions registered through Eventbrite, promoted in its Spring 2026 landlord newsletter alongside a Housing Providers Survey.
CMHA markets the program to owners on the size of the tenant pool: "A large pool of prospective applicants, as CMHA is serves over 15,000 voucher holders."

CMHA's own owner-facing benefit list includes "[s]table, guaranteed rent payments deposited directly into your account," subsidy that increases if the tenant loses income, the ability to use the landlord's own lease and go month-to-month after the initial term, and tenant obligations defined in the Lease Addendum.
CMHA's homepage impact counters state 55,000 people served, $8,666,666 distributed to local landlords monthly, and 4,586 units converted to the RAD program.

Effective January 1, 2026, submitting an RFTA triggers a requirement that the property owner or designated managing agent complete CMHA's Virtual Landlord Orientation — required once every 24 months — and the orientation must be completed before CMHA can execute a HAP contract for a new tenancy.
The 2026 orientation mandate does not disturb existing business: "Existing HAP contracts and current HAP payments are not affected."

CMHA Landlord Orientation is a live virtual session of about 90 minutes (1.5 hours), scheduled and registered through Eventbrite, with attendance verified by CMHA before the HAP contract is executed; CMHA asks landlords to register using the same name and email used for CMHA correspondence.
The landlord does not obtain the RFTA from CMHA — the voucher holder brings it: CMHA's step 4 is "Submit Request for Tenancy Approval Packet (RFTA) provided to you by HCV Family."
The completed RFTA packet is submitted by email to rfta@cmha.net or via the kiosks in CMHA's Main Campus lobby at 8120 Kinsman Road, Cleveland, OH 44104.
Under CMHA policy the RFTA "must be signed by both the family and the owner," and "[t]he owner may submit the RTA on behalf of the family" — so a landlord or property manager can file it directly.
CMHA policy limits a voucher family to one live RFTA: "The family may not submit, and the PHA will not process, more than one (1) RTA at a time."

CMHA reviews the RFTA for completeness and consistency with the proposed lease, and will not accept missing information or corrections by phone — deficiencies must be corrected in writing, a common source of delay.

CMHA's Landlord Participation Guide lists the RFTA packet as containing the Request for Tenancy Approval, HUD Tenancy Addendum, Landlord Fraud Letter, Landlord Certification, Drug-Free Housing Lease Addendum, Lead-based Paint Disclosure, Direct Deposit Form (if applicable), W-9 (new vendors only), and Agent Form (if applicable).
facts.source_name: CMHA — HCV Landlord Participation Guide, Step 3

facts.claim: After the RFTA is submitted CMHA verifies ownership through the County Auditor's website and confirms the unit is not in foreclosure and is current on property taxes.
facts.source_name: CMHA — HCV Landlord Participation Guide, Step 3

facts.claim: CMHA advises landlords to check the voucher's expiration date, which is "180 days from the issue date," and the unit size on the voucher, before proceeding — the RFTA and proposed lease must be submitted no later than the voucher expiration date.
facts.source_name: CMHA — HCV Landlord Participation Guide, Step 2

facts.claim: CMHA must collect a Certificate of Occupancy and/or Rental Registration with the RFTA for units in 17 named suburbs: Bedford, Bedford Heights, Brook Park, Cleveland Heights, Cuyahoga Heights, East Cleveland, Euclid, Fairview Park, Garfield Heights, Lakewood, Lyndhurst, Maple Heights, Parma, Shaker Heights, South Euclid, University Heights and Warrensville Heights.

"Effective October 1, 2025, CMHA will verify active City of Cleveland rental registrations through the City's Citizen Access Portal for units located in Cleveland" — a lapsed Cleveland rental registration will now stall an RFTA.

CMHA policy is to complete the initial inspection, determine whether the unit passes, and notify owner and family "within 15 days of submission of the Request for Tenancy Approval (RTA)," with the clock suspended for any period the unit is not available for inspection.
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, Timing of Initial Inspections (24 CFR 982.305(b)(2))

facts.claim: For the tenancy-approval decision itself, CMHA policy states: "The PHA will complete its determination within 10 business days of receiving all required information."
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, Section 9-I.F Tenancy Approval

facts.claim: If the unit fails, CMHA policy is to "reinspect the unit within five business days of the date the owner notifies the PHA that the required corrections have been made."
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, Inspection Results and Reinspections

facts.claim: An initial inspection gets two attempts: the unit is rescheduled for a second inspection within 7-10 days, and "[w]hen a unit fails an initial inspection the second time, the HCVP applicant/participant is provided with written notice to find another unit."
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, Initial Inspections

facts.claim: The HAP contract "must be executed no later than 60 calendar days from the beginning of the lease term"; CMHA may not pay any HAP before execution, but if executed inside that window it will pay for the portion of the lease term before execution (maximum 60 days). "Any HAP contract executed after the 60-day period is void."
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, Section 9-I.G (24 CFR 982.305)

facts.claim: CMHA "will not execute the HAP contract until the owner has submitted IRS form W-9."

CMHA warns owners not to let a tenant move in before the CMHA-approved date: "If the owner permits the tenant to move into the unit, the tenant will be responsible for the full amount of the rent and HCVP will not pay the back amount retroactively."

The initial lease term must be at least one year, the HAP contract runs concurrently with the lease (when one ends, so does the other), and CMHA will not authorize the family to move during the first year of the lease.
facts.source_name: CMHA — HCV Landlord Participation Guide, Step 6

facts.claim: Direct deposit is mandatory, not optional: "All owners are required to go on direct deposit to receive HAP payments." Owners enroll with the Automatic Deposit form (ACH credit authorization) plus a voided check, mailed to HCVP Finance, 8120 Kinsman Road, Cleveland, Ohio 44104.
CMHA states that HAP "[p]ayment dates are generally on the 3rd and 16th of each month."

CMHA publishes a 2026 HAP Payment Calendar with two runs a month: a Primary HAP Disbursement and a Mid-Month HAP Disbursement for "approved adjustments such as new admissions, contract changes, corrections, or other qualifying payment updates that occur after the primary payment has been processed."

The 2026 primary HAP disbursement dates are Jan 5, Feb 3, Mar 3, Apr 3, May 4, Jun 3, Jul 3, Aug 4, Sep 3, Oct 2, Nov 3 and Dec 3; the mid-month dates are Jan 20, Feb 17, Mar 16, Apr 16, May 15, Jun 15, Jul 15, Aug 17, Sep 15, Oct 15, Nov 16 and Dec 15 — all "subject to change due to holidays or operational needs."

On a new contract, "you will be paid on the next scheduled payment date after HCVP has received three copies of the signed contracts, the executed lease and occupancy permit, when applicable" — returning signed contracts too late simply pushes payment to the next run.

CMHA must make monthly HAP payments to the owner at the beginning of each month, and "[i]f a lease term begins after the first of the month, the HAP payment for the first month is prorated for a partial month."
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, Section 13-II.C HAP Contract Payments

facts.claim: CMHA's owner portal is a RENTCafé-based Landlord Portal reached from applications.cmha.net ("Landlord Login") or directly at applications-cmha.securecafe.com/landlord/cuyahoga-metropolitan-housing-authority/login.aspx; new vendors are issued a unique registration code by CMHA to create the account.

The Landlord Portal gives owners 24-hour access to HAP payment information and the HAP payment calendar, a ledger exportable to Excel and PDF, bank-account updates, 1099s, document upload, tenant caseworker information, inspection history, and failed inspection items with photos within 72 hours of a failed inspection.
facts.source_name: CMHA — HCV Landlord Participation Guide, Step 6

facts.claim: Landlords receive a welcome letter from the Director of the Housing Choice Voucher Program containing a unique vendor/landlord registration code; a registration code is required to create the portal account, and the Tax ID or SSN entered must match the CMHA vendor record.

A buyer acquiring a property with an active HCVP HAP contract must complete and submit CMHA's Change of Ownership packet; "[t]he new owner will begin receiving payments when the packet has been completed and processed."
CMHA's Housing Choice Voucher Program is reachable at (216) 431-1471 (fax (216) 271-4111), at 8120 Kinsman Road, Cleveland, OH 44104; CMHA's main line is 216.348.5000, Monday-Friday 8 a.m. to 5 p.m.
facts.source_name: CMHA HCVP Authorization Agreement for Automatic Deposits (ACH Credits)

facts.claim: CMHA's Housing Choice Voucher payment standards are set per ZIP code (Small Area FMRs), not as a single countywide figure; the agency's Rent Determination tool publishes FY2026 payment standards effective 1/1/2026 for 53 ZIP codes in its service area.
CMHA states its payment standards are based on HUD-established Fair Market Rents and are set between 90% and 110% of the FMRs, or approved exceptions as applicable.

CMHA states it was required to implement Small Area Fair Market Rent payment standards effective January 1, 2025, intended to better reflect local markets and give families more access to low-poverty, high-opportunity areas.

CMHA is explicit that the payment standard is not the rent ceiling: "The payment standard is NOT the maximum amount that the landlord can charge; it is the maximum amount of subsidy that the CMHA will pay toward each tenant's rent portion."
For FY2026 (effective 1/1/2026), CMHA studio/efficiency payment standards range from $802 (ZIP 44127) to $1,582 (ZIPs 44113 and 44040), with a median across the 53 service-area ZIPs of $1,118.
For FY2026 (effective 1/1/2026), CMHA one-bedroom payment standards range from $915 (ZIPs 44104 and 44127) to $1,796 (ZIPs 44113 and 44040), with a median of $1,265.
For FY2026 (effective 1/1/2026), CMHA two-bedroom payment standards range from $1,096 (ZIP 44127) to $2,169 (ZIPs 44113 and 44040), with a median of $1,525.
For FY2026 (effective 1/1/2026), CMHA three-bedroom payment standards range from $1,412 (ZIP 44127) to $2,791 (ZIPs 44113 and 44040), with a median of $1,966.
For FY2026 (effective 1/1/2026), CMHA four-bedroom payment standards range from $1,514 (ZIP 44127) to $2,983 (ZIPs 44113 and 44040), with a median of $2,101.

In ZIP 44113 (Tremont / Ohio City / near West Side), CMHA's FY2026 payment standards effective 1/1/2026 are $1,582 studio, $1,796 one-bedroom, $2,169 two-bedroom, $2,791 three-bedroom, and $2,983 four-bedroom — the highest tier in CMHA's service area.
ZIP 44127 (Slavic Village area) carries CMHA's lowest FY2026 payment standards: $802 studio, $915 one-bedroom, $1,096 two-bedroom, $1,412 three-bedroom, $1,514 four-bedroom.

For FY2026 (effective 1/1/2026) CMHA's two-bedroom payment standard is $1,683 in ZIP 44118 (Cleveland Heights) versus $1,209 in ZIP 44105, and the three-bedroom standard is $2,169 versus $1,559 in the same two ZIPs.
facts.source_name: CMHA Payment Standards FY2026 lookup tool

facts.claim: CMHA's FY2026 two-bedroom payment standard varies by roughly 98% across Cuyahoga County ZIP codes — from $1,096 in 44127 to $2,169 in 44113 — so any single "CMHA payment standard" figure quoted without a ZIP code is misleading.

The payment-standard table CMHA's Rent Determination tool displays before a ZIP code is selected ($881 studio / $1,005 1BR / $1,209 2BR / $1,559 3BR / $1,661 4BR) is not a countywide standard — it corresponds only to ZIP codes 44103 and 44105, and must not be cited as "the CMHA payment standard."

Derived, not published: across all 53 ZIP codes and 5 bedroom sizes (265 combinations), CMHA's FY2026 payment standards compute to about 113% of HUD's FY2026 Small Area FMR for the same ZIP, with every combination falling between 112.9% and 113.0%; CMHA does not publish this relationship and its legal basis is unsourced.
facts.source_name: Computed from CMHA Rent Determination tool vs. HUD FY2026 SAFMR file

facts.claim: HUD's FY2026 Fair Market Rents for the Cleveland, OH HUD Metro FMR Area (which includes Cuyahoga County) are $933 efficiency, $1,058 one-bedroom, $1,279 two-bedroom, $1,646 three-bedroom, and $1,760 four-bedroom.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 FMR County Level Data (revised), huduser.gov

facts.claim: HUD no longer labels this FMR area "Cleveland-Elyria"; the FY2026 area name is "Cleveland, OH HUD Metro FMR Area" (HUD area code METRO17410N17460), covering Cuyahoga, Geauga, Lake, Lorain, and Medina counties.
facts.value: Cleveland, OH HUD Metro FMR Area (METRO17410N17460)
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 FMR County Level Data (revised), huduser.gov

facts.claim: FY2026 Fair Market Rents took effect October 1, 2025 under FMR Notice 90 FR 41096, and HUD later published Revised FY2026 FMRs (91 FR 21301) effective May 21, 2026 — the version currently in force as of July 2026.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — Fair Market Rents (FMRs) dataset page

facts.claim: HUD's May 21, 2026 revision did not change any Cleveland-area figures: the metro FMRs and all 126 Cleveland-area ZIP-level Small Area FMRs are byte-identical between HUD's original FY2026 files and the revised files.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 Small Area FMRs (revised vs. original), huduser.gov

facts.claim: HUD's FY2026 Small Area FMRs for ZIP 44113 (Cleveland, OH HUD Metro FMR Area) are $1,400 studio, $1,590 one-bedroom, $1,920 two-bedroom, $2,470 three-bedroom, and $2,640 four-bedroom.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 Small Area FMRs (revised), huduser.gov

facts.claim: HUD's FY2026 Small Area FMRs for ZIP 44104 are $720 studio, $810 one-bedroom, $980 two-bedroom, $1,260 three-bedroom, and $1,350 four-bedroom.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 Small Area FMRs (revised), huduser.gov

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.507(b), a housing authority must determine whether the rent to owner is reasonable in comparison to rent for other comparable unassisted units, considering (1) the location, quality, size, unit type, and age of the contract unit, and (2) any amenities, housing services, maintenance and utilities to be provided by the owner under the lease.
facts.source_name: 24 CFR 982.507 — Rent to owner: Reasonable rent (eCFR)

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.507(a), a PHA may not approve a lease until it determines the initial rent to owner is reasonable, must redetermine reasonable rent before any rent increase, and the rent to owner may never exceed the most recent reasonableness determination during the assisted tenancy.
facts.source_name: 24 CFR 982.507 — Rent to owner: Reasonable rent (eCFR)

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.507(d), by accepting each monthly housing assistance payment the owner certifies that the rent to owner is not more than the rent charged by that owner for comparable unassisted units in the premises.
facts.source_name: 24 CFR 982.507 — Rent to owner: Reasonable rent (eCFR)

facts.claim: CMHA's live landlord Rent page states that it determines rent reasonableness by comparing the proposed unit to two comparable unassisted units in the area, considering location, amenities, size/type, and utilities.

CMHA's Landlord Participation Guide states that CMHA compares the proposed unit to three unassisted units with similar specifications, weighing location, quality, size, unit type, age, amenities, maintenance, and housing services — contradicting the two-comparable figure on CMHA's live Rent page.

CMHA performs the rent reasonableness determination after the Request for Tenancy Approval is submitted and reviewed; a unit that is not rent reasonable is ineligible for the HCV program, because no federal funds may be used for any part of rent that is not reasonable.
CMHA applies rent reasonableness to gross rent — the sum of the rent charged plus tenant-paid utilities (or the utility allowance) — not to the contract rent alone.

For an initial CMHA HCV contract, the total tenant portion paid directly to the landlord can be no more than 40% of the family's monthly income; if it would exceed that, CMHA negotiates with the owner to reduce the rent.
CMHA calculates Total Tenant Payment as the greater of 30% of monthly adjusted income, 10% of gross monthly income, or CMHA's minimum rent of $50.
CMHA calculates the Housing Assistance Payment as the lesser of (payment standard minus Total Tenant Payment) or (gross rent minus Total Tenant Payment).
If a voucher family selects a unit larger than the bedroom size on its voucher, CMHA applies the payment standard and utility allowance for the smaller of the two (voucher size or actual unit size).

Rent increases are requested by emailing RentAdjustment@cmha.net a form signed by both landlord and tenant; CMHA requires that the HAP contract be at least one year old, that there be a passed annual inspection within 12 months of the adjustment's effective date, a current recertification, at least 12 months since the last approved adjustment, and a rent-reasonable amount.
An approved rent increase takes effect "the 1st of the month after 60 days of receiving the request" — so landlords should file roughly 60-90 days before they want the new rent to start.

As of July 2026, Housing Choice Voucher units are still inspected under Housing Quality Standards (HQS), not NSPIRE — CMHA's landlord inspections page states "HUD has extended the start date for the new NSPIRE Inspection Standards for the Housing Choice Voucher (HCV) Program. The final transition deadline has been moved from October 1, 2025 to February 1, 2027."

HUD's Federal Register notice published September 30, 2025 (document 2025-19070) "further extends the compliance date for HUD's National Standards for the Physical Inspection of Real Estate (NSPIRE) final rule for the Housing Choice Voucher (HCV), Project-Based Voucher (PBV), and Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation (Mod Rehab) programs through January 31, 2027," making NSPIRE mandatory for HCV units on February 1, 2027.

HUD states the September 30, 2025 extension of the NSPIRE compliance date for HCV, PBV and Mod Rehab "is the third extension of this compliance date," following prior extensions to October 1, 2024 and October 1, 2025.

HUD continues to encourage PHAs that are ready to implement NSPIRE to proceed at their earliest convenience — early adoption before the February 1, 2027 deadline is permitted, so a PHA's actual standard must be confirmed with that PHA rather than assumed from the federal deadline.
facts.source_name: HUD — NSPIRE Official Notices and Proposed Rules

facts.claim: 24 CFR 982.401 ("Housing quality standards"), as amended at 89 FR 38296 effective May 7, 2024, now reads: "As defined in § 982.4, HQS refers to the minimum quality standards developed by HUD in accordance with 24 CFR 5.703, including any variations approved by HUD for the PHA under 24 CFR 5.705(a)(3)" — the regulatory text already points to the NSPIRE standards even though the HCV compliance date is deferred to February 1, 2027.

NSPIRE replaces HQS's 13 performance requirements with three inspectable areas — Unit, Inside, and Outside — and under 24 CFR 5.703(a) HCV, PBV and Mod Rehab units are subject to the standards only for the subsidized unit itself plus egress routes, residential common features, and systems equipment directly servicing the unit.

NSPIRE's affirmative requirements under 24 CFR 5.703 include: at least one working smoke detector on each level; carbon monoxide detection meeting HUD standards; GFCI protection for any outlet installed within 6 feet of a water source; a guardrail wherever an elevated walking surface has a drop-off of 30 inches or greater; permanently mounted light fixtures in kitchens and each bathroom; and no unvented gas, oil or kerosene space heaters.

HUD Notice PIH 2024-26 REV-1 states that under NSPIRE, HCV/PBV/Mod Rehab inspections "are not numerically scored and NSPIRE retains pass/fail indicators"; Life-Threatening deficiencies must be corrected within 24 hours of notice, Severe and Moderate within 30 days (or a PHA-approved extension), and Low deficiencies "will result in a pass and would only be noted by the inspector for information purposes."

Per HUD Notice PIH 2024-26 REV-1: "If a failed deficiency (Life-Threatening, Severe, or Moderate) is not corrected within the required timeframe, the PHA must initiate Housing Assistance Payment (HAP) Contract enforcement, which can include withholding or abatement of assistance payments, terminations, or relocations."

HUD Notice PIH 2024-26 REV-1 states the HOTMA life-threatening conditions list, published with the final NSPIRE Standards on June 22, 2023, "will become mandatory for all HCV PHAs at the time of NSPIRE implementation" and "will no longer be discretionary" — it appears in Table 65 of the NSPIRE Standards; PHAs may still add extra LT items via their Administrative Plan.
facts.source_name: HUD Notice PIH 2024-26, REV-1 (quoting the NSPIRE Standards, 88 FR 40832)

facts.claim: Per HUD Notice PIH 2024-26 REV-1, "24 CFR 5.707 exempts landlords participating in HCV, PBV, and Section 8 Mod Rehab programs from any self-inspection requirements" — the self-inspection duty that applies to other NSPIRE programs does not apply to voucher landlords.

HUD Notice PIH 2024-26 REV-1 reminds PHAs and owners that "any deteriorated paint in target housing, or other lead-based paint hazard identified through a lead-based paint risk assessment or lead-based paint inspection is considered a violation of HQS and the NSPIRE Standards," with 24 CFR Part 35 Subpart M applying to tenant-based units where a child under age 6 resides or is expected to reside.

CMHA's Inspections page describes four HCV inspection types — Initial (on submission of an RFTA packet), Annual ("within twelve months of the previous inspection"), Special ("in response to complaints registered with the PHA") and Emergency — and says CMHA is looking to transition to biennial inspections; treat the frequency as unsettled, because CMHA's landlord index page markets "Biennial inspections" and its Landlord Participation Guide also says biennial.

CMHA states its HQS inspections are conducted by an outside firm that ensures potential and current HCV units meet the minimum performance and acceptability criteria for each of the 13 key housing quality aspects.

For CMHA's periodic, emergency and special inspections, the Landlord Participation Guide states "Up to three inspections are allowed" — a wider margin than the two-visit limit on initial move-in inspections.

CMHA publishes this list of violations that must be corrected within 24 hours: a smoke or carbon monoxide detector that is missing or inoperable; kitchen range burners that do not ignite; a hazardous gas hook-up for a kitchen range evidenced by a strong smell of gas; no water; no electricity; no heat (October 15 to April 15); sewer back-up; gas leak; security issues (unlockable windows within six feet of ground level and/or unit entry doors with missing or broken locks); and anything deemed life-threatening.

Effective December 29, 2024, CMHA requires one smoke detector on each level, one inside each bedroom/sleeping area, one within 21 feet of the door to each bedroom, and one in any living area separated from bedrooms by a door; all battery-operated detectors must be 10-year sealed/tamper-resistant, or hardwired.

Carbon monoxide detectors have been required since December 27, 2022 where there is any gas-fueled appliance, an attached garage, or a wood-burning fireplace, and "[l]ack of carbon monoxide detector(s) will be considered an emergency fail subject to correction within 24 hours."

For pre-1978 units cited for defective paint where a child under 6 will reside, the owner has 10 days from the initial move-in inspection (and 30 days from an annual inspection) to provide a passed lead clearance test and proof repairs were done by a certified lead abatement contractor.
facts.source_name: CMHA — Inspections (EPA Lead Law and HCVP)

facts.claim: CMHA inspection contacts: schedule or request an inspection at 216.478.9680; inspection help at 216.271.2116; RFTA submissions to rfta@cmha.net; HCV main office 8120 Kinsman Rd, Cleveland, OH 44104, (216) 431-1471.

Under 24 CFR 982.405(b), "The PHA must inspect the unit at least biennially during assisted occupancy" (small rural PHAs, once every three years) — biennial is the federal minimum, and a PHA may inspect more often.

Under 24 CFR 982.405(d), when a tenant or government official reports a deficiency, the PHA must inspect and notify the owner within 24 hours if life-threatening (owner then repairs within 24 hours), or within 15 days if non-life-threatening (owner then repairs within 30 days or a PHA-approved extension).
facts.value: 24 hours (LT) / 15 days to inspect, 30 days to repair (non-LT)

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.404(a)(3): "If a deficiency is life-threatening, the owner must correct the deficiency within 24 hours of notification. For other deficiencies, the owner must correct the deficiency within 30 calendar days of notification (or any reasonable PHA-approved extension)."

Under 24 CFR 982.405(h), a PHA may not charge the owner for the initial inspection or the first inspection during occupancy, but "may establish a reasonable fee to owners for a reinspection" when a cited deficiency was not corrected — and "The owner may not pass this fee along to the family."
facts.value: reinspection fee allowed; cannot be passed to tenant

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.405(i), to verify a correction the PHA "may use verification methods other than another on-site inspection" and "may accept photographic evidence or other reliable evidence from the owner to verify that a deficiency has been corrected."

Under 24 CFR 982.404(d)(1), a PHA may withhold assistance payments after written notice of deficiencies, and if the unit is brought into compliance during the cure period the PHA "Must resume assistance payments" and "Must provide assistance payments to cover the time period for which the assistance payments were withheld" — money withheld during the cure window is recoverable.
facts.value: withheld HAP is paid retroactively if cured in time

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.404(d)(2)(i), "The PHA must abate the HAP, including amounts that had been withheld, if the owner fails to make the repairs within the applicable cure period" — and 24 CFR 982.4 defines Abatement as "Stopping HAP payments to an owner with no potential for retroactive payment," so abated money is permanently lost.
facts.value: abatement = permanent loss, no retroactive payment

facts.claim: Under 24 CFR 982.404(d)(2)(ii), once a PHA abates payments it must notify the family and owner "that if the unit does not meet HQS within 60 days (or a reasonable longer period established by the PHA) after the determination of noncompliance ... the PHA will terminate the HAP contract for the unit, and the family will have to move," and the PHA must issue the family its voucher to move at least 30 days prior to termination.

Under 24 CFR 982.404(d)(3), "An owner may not terminate the tenancy of any family due to the withholding or abatement of assistance" — a landlord whose HAP is abated for their own failed repairs cannot evict the tenant on that basis.

CMHA states: "CMHA may abate HAP Payments to owners who do not comply with notifications to correct HQS deficiencies within the specified timeframe. 24 hours or 30 days depending on the nature of the deficiency," and "If payments are abated, the family is still responsible for providing their share of the rent; however, CMHA will not make their payment until the deficiencies have been corrected. There will be no retroactive payments made during that period."
facts.value: 24 hours or 30 days; no retroactive payment

facts.claim: CMHA's Landlord FAQ states "Rent is abated when the unit fails to pass on two consecutive inspections," that if the violation is uncorrected at the second inspection "the HAP will abate on the first of the month following the failure to comply," that during abatement the family still owes its portion while CMHA pays nothing with no retroactive payments, and that "[i]f a unit is in abatement for more than 30 days, the HAP Contract may be terminated."
facts.value: 2 consecutive failures; abates 1st of following month; 30 days

facts.claim: The only publicly advertised cash landlord bonus in Cuyahoga County is through the Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration: "Property owners who lease up with a Community Choice Demonstration client will receive a bonus in the amount of 50% of one month's rent."
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Owners

facts.claim: The same program offers owners "a Damage Mitigation Fund of up to $2,000 in the rare event that a tenant leaves their unit damaged," plus tenant security-deposit assistance, pre- and post-move coaching for the family, help completing and expediting the paperwork at the housing authority, and a free exclusive unit-listing service.
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Owners

facts.claim: The Community Choice Demonstration serves CMHA voucher holders specifically — "Families with vouchers from the Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority (CMHA) can sign up to be selected for the program" — and is limited to units in designated "Opportunity Areas" of Cuyahoga County, funded as part of a national HUD demonstration; owners can check eligibility with the site's Address Locator Tool or by calling (216) 770-4048.

CMHA participates in the Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration, under which the payment standard depends on the location of the unit within Cuyahoga County and an Address Locator Tool shows which tier a property falls in and the estimated rental price limit for that neighborhood.

Outside that program, CMHA itself pays nothing for tenant damage: "HCVP does not pay for tenant caused damages." Landlords must bill the tenant, use the security deposit, or pursue the tenant in court.

CMHA's Administrative Plan authorizes — but does not commit to a published amount — owner incentive and/or retention payments for Emergency Housing Voucher lease-ups: "The PHA may make incentive or retention payments to owners that agree to initially lease their unit to an EHV family and/or renew the lease of an EHV family," paid as a single payment at the start of the lease term and not counted as rent or in rent reasonableness.
facts.source_name: CMHA HCV Administrative Plan, EHV Tenant Services Plan

facts.claim: Side deals are prohibited: "Families may not pay a rent share greater than the amount determined by the CMHA. Side deals between the landlord and family are illegal and could result in the family being terminated and the landlord disbarred, suspended or subject to a limited denial of participation."

During an eviction CMHA will hold HAP on the landlord's request, and "[i]f the tenant remains in the unit during the eviction process, and the unit is not in abatement, the landlord may receive all withheld HAP payments, whether or not the landlord won or lost the eviction." Landlords and tenants must give HCVP a copy of any eviction notice.
Ohio has no statewide source-of-income protection: the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights' Ohio Advisory Committee brief states flatly that "Source-of-income discrimination is legal in most of Ohio."
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee — Source-of-Income Housing Discrimination in Ohio (Report Brief)

facts.claim: Ohio's fair housing statute, R.C. 4112.02(H), protects only "race, color, religion, sex, military status, familial status, ancestry, disability, or national origin" — source of income, housing vouchers and public assistance appear nowhere in the section.
facts.value: 9 protected classes; source of income not among them
facts.source_name: Ohio Revised Code § 4112.02 (codes.ohio.gov), eff. April 6, 2023 (HB 281, 134th GA)

facts.claim: The federal Fair Housing Act protects race, color, national origin, sex, religion, disability and familial status, but "it does not prohibit landlords from refusing to accept tenant applications based on a tenant's lawful income source."
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee — Report Brief, July 2025 (citing 42 U.S.C. §§ 3601-3631)

facts.claim: The City of Cleveland does not protect source of income: the City's own Office of Fair Housing lists its protected classes as "Age, Ancestry, Color, Disability, Ethnic group, Familial status, Gender identity, Marital Status, National origin, Race, Religion, Sex, Sexual orientation" — source of income, housing vouchers and Section 8 are absent.
facts.value: 13 protected classes; source of income not among them
facts.source_name: City of Cleveland — Fair Housing (Department of Community Development)

facts.claim: PRRAC's national compilation of source-of-income laws, updated March 2026, lists 23 Ohio municipalities with local protections — Athens, Akron, Bexley, Chauncey, Cincinnati, Cleveland Heights, Columbus, Dayton, Gahanna, Grandview Heights, Linndale, Lorain, Reynoldsburg, South Euclid, Toledo, University Heights, Upper Arlington, Warrensville Heights, Westerville, Whitehall, Wickliffe, Worthington and Yellow Springs — and the City of Cleveland is not among them.
facts.source_name: PRRAC, Appendix B: State, Local, and Federal Laws Barring Source of Income Discrimination (updated March 2026)

facts.claim: As of July 2026 it is lawful in the City of Cleveland for a landlord to refuse a Housing Choice Voucher solely because it is a voucher: no federal law, no Ohio statute (R.C. 4112.02(H)) and no Cleveland ordinance makes source of income a protected class in the city.
facts.value: lawful in the City of Cleveland
facts.source_name: PRRAC Appendix B (March 2026), cross-checked against R.C. 4112.02(H) and the City of Cleveland Fair Housing page

facts.claim: Five Cuyahoga County municipalities ban source-of-income discrimination: Cleveland Heights (Ord. 25-2021, April 19, 2021), University Heights (Ch. 820, enacted April 2, 2012, amended April 15, 2019), South Euclid (Ch. 1408, April 9, 2018), Warrensville Heights (Ch. 113, May 15, 2012) and Linndale (Ch. 515, May 1, 2012).
facts.source_name: PRRAC Appendix B (March 2026), Ohio city entries

facts.claim: Cleveland Heights bans source-of-income discrimination under Ordinance No. 25-2021, enacted April 19, 2021, which amended Codified Ordinances § 749.03(ii) to define "Source of Income" as expressly "including but not limited to housing vouchers, emergency rental assistance, disability benefits, and military or veterans benefits."
facts.source_name: PRRAC Appendix B (March 2026), Cleveland Heights entry quoting Codified Ordinances §§ 749.03, 749.07, 749.08

facts.claim: A Cleveland Heights source-of-income violation is enforced by the Cleveland Heights Fair Practices Board, and Codified Ordinance § 749.19 allows injunctive relief, actual damages, civil penalties up to $10,000 and reasonable attorney's fees.
facts.source_name: PRRAC Appendix B (March 2026), Cleveland Heights entry

facts.claim: Of the five Cuyahoga County cities with source-of-income laws, Signal Cleveland reports that only three — Cleveland Heights, South Euclid and University Heights — specifically include voucher protections.
facts.source_name: Signal Cleveland — "Eight Northeast Ohio cities ban housing income discrimination" (Aug 5, 2024; updated Dec 26, 2024)

facts.claim: There is no Cuyahoga County-wide source-of-income protection; only five of the county's roughly 40 municipalities have passed one, and a 2021 county-level proposal never reached council.

R.C. 5321.19, as amended by HB 430 (134th GA) effective September 23, 2022, bars any political subdivision from enacting or enforcing a measure "that is in conflict with this chapter, or that regulates the rights and obligations of parties to a rental agreement that are regulated by this chapter, including, without limitation, by any way imposing or requiring rent control or rent stabilization" — the statute does not mention source of income.

Ohio is not one of the seven states PRRAC identifies as having preemptive bans on local source-of-income protections — those are Florida, Idaho, Indiana, Iowa, Kentucky, Missouri and Texas — so existing Ohio municipal ordinances remain in force.
facts.value: 7 preemption states; Ohio not among them
facts.source_name: PRRAC Appendix B (March 2026), Section C: States with Preemptive Bans

facts.claim: Ohio House Bill 135 (136th General Assembly), which would amend R.C. 4112.01 and 4112.02 to make "lawful source of income" a protected class statewide, was introduced February 25, 2025 and referred to the Community Revitalization Committee on March 5, 2025; as of July 2026 it has had no further action and has not passed the House.
facts.source_name: Ohio House of Representatives — HB 135 Status (136th GA)

facts.claim: University Heights' source-of-income ordinance is under active legal attack: Crain's Cleveland Business reported on October 31, 2025 that the city's "housing voucher ordinance may face a constitutional challenge," tied to a discrimination suit over the Huntington Green apartments.

Advertising "No Section 8" carries federal Fair Housing Act exposure even where source-of-income discrimination is legal: a 2017 Cuyahoga County audit found housing providers advertising "No section 8" were 26% more likely to deliver unfavorable treatment to Black prospective tenants.
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee report (Oct 2024), p.12, citing Lepley & Mangiarelli (2017)

facts.claim: In Cuyahoga County, 89% of Housing Choice Voucher recipients are Black, versus 60% of Ohio's HCV participants statewide and 13% of Ohio's population — which is why voucher-based screening rules can create racial disparate-impact liability.
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee report (Oct 2024), p.10, citing HUD Picture of Subsidized Households

facts.claim: Only about 50% of households admitted to the voucher program in Cuyahoga County found housing with their voucher before it expired, per CMHA testimony to the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights.
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee report (Oct 2024), p.8 (Wade Testimony, CMHA)

facts.claim: Landlord scarcity is real: "Only about 40% of CMHA voucher holders find housing within six months of getting the subsidy, according to the agency," with nearly 14,000 vouchers in use and another 1,900 available at the time of reporting.
facts.source_name: Signal Cleveland — How one effort is getting people with housing vouchers into homes

facts.claim: There are approximately 15,000 voucher holders in Cuyahoga County and an additional 20,000 households on the CMHA waiting list.
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee report (Oct 2024), p.7 (Wade Testimony, CMHA data)

facts.claim: R.C. 5321.04(A) imposes ten non-waivable landlord duties, including complying with all applicable building, housing, health and safety codes that materially affect health and safety; making all repairs to keep the premises "in a fit and habitable condition"; keeping common areas safe and sanitary; maintaining electrical, plumbing, sanitary, heating, ventilating and air-conditioning fixtures in good working order; supplying running water, reasonable hot water and reasonable heat; and arranging waste removal for buildings with four or more units.
facts.source_name: Ohio Revised Code § 5321.04 (codes.ohio.gov), eff. Sept 28, 2012 (HB 490, 129th GA)

facts.claim: Under R.C. 5321.04(A)(8) a landlord must give reasonable notice before entering a tenant's unit, and "Twenty-four hours is presumed to be a reasonable notice" absent an emergency.

R.C. 5321.16(A): "Any security deposit in excess of fifty dollars or one month's periodic rent, whichever is greater, shall bear interest on the excess at the rate of five per cent per annum if the tenant remains in possession of the premises for six months or more," and that interest must be computed and paid annually to the tenant.

R.C. 5321.16(B) requires that any deduction from a security deposit "be itemized and identified by the landlord in a written notice delivered to the tenant together with the amount due, within thirty days after termination of the rental agreement and delivery of possession."

R.C. 5321.16(C): a landlord who misses the 30-day itemized-return requirement owes the tenant the money due "together with damages in an amount equal to the amount wrongfully withheld, and reasonable attorneys fees" — effectively double the withheld amount plus fees.
facts.value: double the amount withheld + attorney fees

facts.claim: Ohio sets no statutory maximum on the size of a residential security deposit — R.C. 5321.16 regulates only interest on the excess above the greater of $50 or one month's rent, and the 30-day itemized return; it contains no cap. This is an inference from statutory silence, not an affirmative statement by any government source.
facts.source_name: Ohio Revised Code § 5321.16 (codes.ohio.gov) — statute is silent on any maximum

facts.claim: R.C. 1923.04 requires a landlord to serve a notice to leave the premises "three or more days before beginning the action," and for residential evictions the notice must carry this exact language conspicuously: "You are being asked to leave the premises. If you do not leave, an eviction action may be initiated against you. If you are in doubt regarding your legal rights and obligations as a tenant, it is recommended that you seek legal assistance."
facts.source_name: Ohio Revised Code § 1923.04 (codes.ohio.gov), eff. Oct 18, 2007 (HB 56, 127th GA)

facts.claim: R.C. 5321.17 requires at least 7 days' notice to terminate a week-to-week tenancy and at least 30 days' notice before the periodic rental date to terminate a month-to-month tenancy.

R.C. 1923.06 requires that service of the eviction summons "be at least seven days before the day set for trial," and the restitution claim "shall be scheduled for hearing in accordance with local court rules, but in no event sooner than the seventh day from the date service is complete."
facts.value: minimum 7 days between service and hearing

facts.claim: R.C. 1923.08 caps eviction continuances: "No continuance in an action under this chapter shall be granted for a period longer than eight days," unless the plaintiff asks and the defendant consents, or the defendant asks and posts a court-approved bond for accruing rent.

R.C. 1923.14(A) requires the bailiff, sheriff, constable or police officer to execute the writ of restitution "within ten days after receiving a writ of execution" by restoring the landlord to possession, unless a stay of execution and bond are filed on appeal.

Cleveland Housing Court states a simple non-payment eviction takes under five weeks: serve the three-day notice and wait three business days, file the complaint, "The court date is set for three weeks from the date of filing," and if granted "the move-out may take place within seven days from the date of hearing" — but the same answer warns that cases involving tenant misconduct, termination of a periodic tenancy, or "tenants on whose behalf the landlord receives a rent subsidy may take longer."
facts.source_name: Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division — Frequently Asked Questions: Eviction (rev. Apr-2021)

facts.claim: Cleveland Housing Court gives evicted tenants "between seven and ten days to move from the premises"; most magistrates will extend the move-out to ten days if the household includes children, an elderly parent or another individual with special needs, "however, this extension is not automatic."
facts.source_name: Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division — Eviction FAQ (rev. Apr-2021)

facts.claim: Cleveland Housing Court warns that evicting a voucher tenant is procedurally different: subsidized tenants "are afforded special protection under Federal law," some are entitled to written notice of an opportunity to meet with management before filing, "[m]ost also are entitled to an opportunity to correct their conduct before the landlord may file an eviction action," and the court encourages subsidized landlords to consult an attorney before filing.
facts.source_name: Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division — Eviction FAQ, Q5 (rev. Apr-2021)

facts.claim: Self-help eviction is illegal in Ohio: a landlord "cannot physically remove the tenant from the premises, terminate utilities, or change the locks to encourage a tenant to move from the premises," and must file a complaint, obtain a judgment and follow the court's move-out procedure.
facts.source_name: Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division — Eviction FAQ, Q1 (rev. Apr-2021)

facts.claim: An Ohio tenant may not simply withhold rent for unmade repairs: the tenant must give the landlord written notice of the defective condition and, if repairs are not made in a reasonable time, deposit the rent with the court under the R.C. 5321.07 rent-deposit process.
facts.value: rent escrow with court, not withholding
facts.source_name: Cleveland Municipal Court, Housing Division — Eviction FAQ, Q6 (rev. Apr-2021)

facts.claim: Cleveland requires owners of rental units built before 1978 to hold a Lead Safe Certification or an exemption; the certification is valid for two years, after which owners must re-apply, and owner-occupied properties do not need to be certified.
facts.source_name: City of Cleveland, Department of Building & Housing — Lead Safe Certification: Landlord/Owner Responsibilities

facts.claim: Under Cleveland's Residents First rules, owners of non-owner-occupied residential property must register and pay registration fees for each property, and submit an affidavit naming a local agent in charge, proof of current taxes or an established payment plan, and lead-safe certification, in order to obtain a Certificate Approving Rental Occupancy; "[f]ailure to register rental properties as required may result in penalties, fines, and possible criminal prosecution."
facts.value: registration + local agent in charge + lead-safe cert

facts.claim: Cleveland's Residents First law requires every landlord to name a "local agent" based in Cuyahoga County who takes legal responsibility for the property, and is enforced with $200 civil tickets whose unpaid fines are added to the property tax bill; as of mid-June 2025, landlords representing 10,314 properties had registered 2,433 local agents.
facts.source_name: Signal Cleveland — "How is Cleveland's 'Residents First' landlord law going so far?" (June 25, 2025)

facts.claim: A Cleveland property management firm's own published guide states that monthly management fees in Cleveland "generally range between eight and twelve percent of collected monthly rent."
facts.source_name: B2B Property Management (Brooklyn, OH) — "The Real Cost of Property Management in Cleveland"

facts.claim: IIP Management, a Northeast Ohio property manager, publishes that "most property management companies in this region charge between 8% to 12% of the monthly rent as a management fee."
facts.source_name: IIP Management (Cleveland, OH) — Property Management Fees

facts.claim: Progressive Urban Property Management (4001 Detroit Ave, Cleveland) publishes a management fee of 10% per month (or $80 minimum) for single-family and 1–3 unit properties, and 8% per month (or $60 minimum) for 4–9 unit properties.

High Tech Property Management (Brook Park, serving West Cleveland) publishes three tiers: Bronze at 8% monthly ($79 minimum), Silver at 11% monthly ($109 minimum), and Gold at 12% monthly ($139 minimum).
B2B Property Management (4320 Ridge Rd, Brooklyn, OH) publishes a Standard plan at 5% of rent ("minimums apply") and a Premium plan at $129 per unit after bulk discounts.
facts.source_name: B2B Property Management — Transparent Property Management Pricing

facts.claim: Realty Trust Services (3407 West 117th St, Cleveland) publishes a "simple flat rate monthly fee" of $129/month for property management, with volume pricing available.

Evernest's Cleveland office is listed by an aggregator as charging 8%–10% of monthly rent (capped at $199) depending on service plan and unit count; Evernest's own pricing page does not disclose the percentage.
facts.source_name: iPropertyManagement — Best Property Management Companies in Cleveland, OH

facts.claim: A Cleveland property manager's published fee guide states tenant placement fees in Cleveland "typically range from fifty to one hundred percent of one month's rent."
facts.source_name: B2B Property Management — "The Real Cost of Property Management in Cleveland"

facts.claim: Evernest Cleveland publishes a leasing fee of 50% of one month's rent, with a $500 minimum.

Progressive Urban Property Management publishes a leasing fee of one full month's rent on every managed plan, with an $800 minimum on tenant-placement-only, plus an additional $250 for voucher tenants and a $100–$150 per-unit one-time setup fee.
B2B Property Management publishes a new tenant placement commission of one month's rent on its Standard 5% plan (included at no extra charge on its Premium plan).
Realty Trust Services publishes a tenant-placement leasing fee of one month's rent.
A Cleveland property manager's published fee guide states lease renewal fees in Cleveland "typically range from one hundred to three hundred dollars per renewal."
facts.source_name: B2B Property Management — "The Real Cost of Property Management in Cleveland"

facts.claim: Evernest Cleveland publishes a lease renewal fee of $250 when a tenant re-signs their lease.
High Tech Property Management publishes lease renewal fees of $295 (Bronze), $249 (Silver), and $149 (Gold) depending on plan.
Realty Trust Services publishes a lease renewal (resident retention) fee of $199 per renewal.
B2B Property Management publishes a lease renewal charge of $195 per renewal on its Standard plan (included on Premium), and an eviction fee of $195 per filing.

iPropertyManagement's survey of property management companies across 80 metropolitan areas found an average management fee of 8.49% of total monthly rent, with advertised rates ranging from 3.75% to 14.0%, and an average flat fee of $101.04 per unit per month.
facts.source_name: iPropertyManagement — Average Property Management Fees (80-metro survey)

facts.claim: The same 80-metro survey found tenant placement averages 70.6% of one month's rent when bundled with management services and 81.8% when sold as a standalone service.
facts.source_name: iPropertyManagement — Average Property Management Fees (80-metro survey)

facts.claim: The U.S. Census Bureau's Housing Vacancy Survey puts the Cleveland, OH metro rental vacancy rate at 8.1% in the first quarter of 2026, with a margin of error of ±4.0 percentage points.
facts.source_name: U.S. Census Bureau, HVS Table 4 — Rental Vacancy Rates for the 75 Largest MSAs: 2026

facts.claim: Census HVS quarterly rental vacancy rates for the Cleveland-Elyria, OH metro in 2025 were 6.0%, 3.3%, 4.4% and 11.7% (Q1–Q4), swinging by more than 8 points across the year with margins of error of ±3.0 to ±5.5 points.
facts.source_name: U.S. Census Bureau, HVS Table 4b — Rental Vacancy Rates for the 75 Largest MSAs: 2015 to 2025

facts.claim: Yardi Matrix's April 2026 Cleveland Multifamily Market Report puts the metro's occupancy rate for stabilized assets at 94.5% as of February 2026, with average advertised asking rents of $1,246 (up 2.8% year-over-year).
facts.source_name: Yardi Matrix — Cleveland Multifamily Market Report, April 2026

facts.claim: Colliers data reported in April 2026 put Cleveland apartment occupancy at 95.8% in Q1 2026, with about 7,533 vacant units and an average monthly apartment rent of $1,419.
facts.source_name: NEOtrans — "Cleveland has Ohio's highest apartment rents" (citing Colliers/MSCI)

facts.claim: RentCafe's Rental Competitiveness Index reports that U.S. apartments took an average of 46 days to fill with occupancy at 92.7% in early 2026 — a national figure; Cleveland is not among the 139 markets ranked.
facts.source_name: RentCafe — Rental Competitiveness Index (Yardi Systems data, Oct–Dec 2025)

facts.claim: A Cleveland property manager's published guide states that "a realistic vacancy estimate for most single-family properties is two to four weeks annually" in the Cleveland market.
facts.source_name: B2B Property Management — "The Real Cost of Property Management in Cleveland"

facts.claim: IIP Management, a Cleveland-area property manager, advertises a "39-Day Guarantee" on tenant placement — the only published Cleveland-specific time-to-lease benchmark found.
Derived, not published: at HUD's FY2026 two-bedroom Fair Market Rent of $1,279 for the Cleveland metro, a single vacant day costs a landlord roughly $42 in lost gross rent ($1,279 ÷ 30 = $42.63).
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 Fair Market Rents (arithmetic derivation)

facts.claim: Derived, not published: at Yardi Matrix's February 2026 Cleveland average advertised asking rent of $1,246, a single vacant day costs roughly $41 in lost gross rent ($1,246 ÷ 30 = $41.53).
facts.source_name: Yardi Matrix — Cleveland Multifamily Market Report, April 2026 (arithmetic derivation)

facts.claim: Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority (PHA code OH003) appears on HUD's official Mandatory SAFMR PHA list with a designation year of 2024, meaning CMHA is required — not merely permitted — to use Small Area Fair Market Rents to set Housing Choice Voucher payment standards.
facts.source_name: HUD Office of Public and Indian Housing — Mandatory SAFMR PHA List (PDF)

facts.claim: The Cleveland-Elyria, OH MSA is explicitly named in HUD's Federal Register FY2025 Fair Market Rent notice as one of 41 additional metropolitan areas required to use Small Area Fair Market Rents in the Housing Choice Voucher program beginning in FY 2025, pursuant to the October 25, 2023 notice at 88 FR 73352.
facts.source_name: Federal Register, 89 FR 66131 — Fair Market Rents for the Housing Choice Voucher Program … Fiscal Year 2025 (govinfo.gov)

facts.claim: HUD states there are currently 65 metropolitan areas where use of SAFMRs is required — the 24 designated by the November 16, 2016 final rule plus the 41 added by the October 25, 2023 notice (which includes Cleveland-Elyria).
facts.source_name: HUD.gov — Small Area Fair Market Rents

facts.claim: CMHA states on its own landlord rent page that "Effective January 1, 2025, CMHA is required to Implement Small Area Fair Market Rent Payment Standards," which "allow for payment standards to be established that more accurately reflect the local market and are intended to provide families with more access to low-poverty, high-opportunity areas."

CMHA's Housing Choice Voucher payment standards effective January 1, 2026 are set by ZIP code (Small Area FMR-based) and range for a 2-bedroom from $1,096 in ZIP 44127 to $2,169 in ZIPs 44040 and 44113 — a spread of roughly 2x across Cuyahoga County.
facts.source_name: CMHA Rent Determination tool (apps.cmha.net) — embedded as an iframe on cmha.net/housing/landlords/rent.php

facts.claim: CMHA's FY2026 payment standards equal exactly 113% of HUD's published FY2026 Small Area FMR for every one of the 53 Cuyahoga ZIP codes across bedroom sizes 0-4 (e.g. ZIP 44113: SAFMR 2BR $1,920 x 1.13 = $2,169) — above the 90-110% basic range that CMHA's own website describes, implying CMHA operates under a HUD-approved exception payment standard.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 Small Area FMRs (revised) data file, cross-computed against apps.cmha.net/rentdetermination

facts.claim: For FY2026 HUD lists all 53 CMHA ZIP codes under the FMR area "Cleveland, OH HUD Metro FMR Area" (HUD area code METRO17410N17460) — a rename from the "Cleveland-Elyria, OH MSA" label used in the FY2025 designation notice, so marketing copy should not treat the two names as different programs.

facts.source_name: HUD USER — FY2026 Small Area FMRs (revised) data file, cross-checked against apps.cmha.net/rentdetermination

facts.claim: The narrative heading on CMHA's landlord rent page still reads "Fair Market Rents and Payment Standards Fiscal Year 2025" and cites the January 1, 2025 SAFMR start date, while the payment-standard tool embedded on that same page serves FY2026 figures effective 1/1/2026 — so the page's prose is a year stale relative to the live data.

CMHA publishes an HCVP utility allowance schedule (Form HUD-52667 equivalent) titled "Housing Allowances for Tenant Furnished Utilities and Other Services — Natural Gas, Electric, Electric Heat Pump," effective January 1, 2026, with rates revised September 2025.
facts.source_name: CMHA — HCVP Utility Allowance Chart, "UA 2026.pdf" (linked from cmha.net/housing/landlords/rent.php)

facts.claim: CMHA's January 1, 2026 utility allowance schedule applies identical water/sewer allowances to City of Cleveland, City of East Cleveland and Suburban units ($98 for 0 BR up to $649 for 8 BR), but differentiates trash: $9/month in the City of Cleveland versus $16/month suburban, at every bedroom size.

CMHA states that "the payment standard is NOT the maximum amount that the landlord can charge; it is the maximum amount of subsidy that the CMHA will pay toward each tenant's rent portion" — a distinction landlord-facing marketing must preserve.

CMHA's published policy language states that "CMHA has established payment standards between 90% and 110% of the FMRs or approved exceptions as applicable" — the "approved exceptions" clause is the only public hook for the 113%-of-SAFMR figure actually in force for 2026.

For an initial CMHA HCV contract the total tenant portion may be no more than 40% of the family's monthly adjusted income; if the family share exceeds that, CMHA will negotiate with the owner to reduce the rent, and the requested rent must separately pass a rent reasonableness test against comparable unassisted units.

CMHA's Housing Choice Voucher waiting list operates as an ongoing lottery with no closing date, with preliminary applications staying active 18 months, and 2025 income limits set at $34,800 for a 1-person household rising to $65,650 for 8 persons (effective 4/03/2025).

facts.claim: CMHA pays Housing Assistance Payments to landlords on the 3rd and 16th of each month, and for a new contract pays "on the next scheduled payment date after HCVP has received three copies of the signed contracts, the executed lease and occupancy permit."

CMHA does not reimburse landlords for tenant-caused damage — its landlord FAQ directs owners to bill the tenant directly or "take the amount from the security deposit," and rent is abated with no retroactive payment if a unit fails two consecutive inspections, with contract termination possible after 30 days of abatement.

CMHA states that families may not pay a rent share greater than the amount CMHA determines, and that "side deals between the landlord and family are illegal and could result in the family being terminated and the landlord disbarred, suspended or subject to a limited denial of participation" — a compliance point any landlord-facing marketing must not contradict.

CMHA's published pitch to landlords is non-cash: direct-deposit HAP payments, access to over 15,000 participants, pre-screened tenants subject to criminal background checks, subsidy increases if a tenant loses income, the ability to use the landlord's own lease and go month-to-month after the initial term, biennial inspections, and access to a Landlord Outreach Coordinator.
facts.source_name: CMHA — Housing Choice Voucher Program Landlord Participation Guide, "Why Should You Partner With CMHA?" (p.4)

facts.claim: CMHA itself publishes NO signing bonus, no damage-mitigation or risk-mitigation fund, and no vacancy loss payment for ordinary Housing Choice Voucher landlords — none appear anywhere in its Landlord FAQ, Leasing with CMHA page, Landlord Participation Guide, Forms & Documents index, or its Winter 2026 and Spring 2026 landlord newsletters. The word "vacancy" does not appear once in the Landlord Participation Guide.
facts.source_name: CMHA — Landlord FAQ (cross-checked against Leasing with CMHA, Landlord Participation Guide, Forms & Documents, Winter/Spring 2026 newsletters)

facts.claim: The frequently-repeated line that CMHA offers landlords "expedited processing and financial benefits as well as damage mitigation funds" comes from a September 25, 2024 Signal Cleveland article, where it describes HUD programs CMHA said it *may* tap — it is not a published, named, currently-open CMHA-wide incentive program, and no dollar amounts or program name were given.
facts.source_name: Signal Cleveland — "How one effort is getting people with housing vouchers into homes" (Sept 25, 2024)

facts.claim: On August 14, 2025 CMHA asked Greater Cleveland landlords to forgo rent increases because of a projected voucher budget shortfall; Dorivette Nolan, CMHA chief of policy, planning and voucher administration, wrote that "to preserve subsidy payment to landlords like you and prevent the termination of Housing Assistance Payment (HAP) contracts... we are implementing various cost-saving measures." This is important context: CMHA is cutting costs, not funding new landlord bonuses.
facts.source_name: Signal Cleveland — "CMHA urges Cleveland landlords to hold rent for Section 8 tenants" (Aug 14, 2025)

facts.claim: CMHA has partnered with AffordableHousing.com and OhioHousingLocator.org to give owners free rental listings, and advises landlords advertising elsewhere to state that "vouchers are welcome."

There is no CMHA landlord application or pre-approval process — the tenant's RFTA packet is submitted by email to rfta@cmha.net or via kiosks in CMHA's Main Campus lobby, after which CMHA makes a rent determination, schedules the HQS inspection, and issues the HAP contract for signature.

Per CMHA's Landlord Participation Guide, the Request for Tenancy Approval packet the family hands the landlord includes the Request for Tenancy Approval form and the HUD Tenancy Addendum; the guide reproduces the HUD-52646 voucher form (07/2019 edition) and sample RFTA, HAP and Tenancy Addendum forms as appendices.

facts.claim: CMHA runs a landlord/owner portal on RentCafe at https://applications-cmha.securecafe.com/landlord/cuyahoga-metropolitan-housing-authority/login.aspx where landlords "view information related to payments and inspections, update contact information, request Electronic Fund Transfer changes and more"; registration requires a unique vendor/landlord registration code mailed by the HCVP Director, and new vendors are issued a code after their first HAP contract.

Effective January 1, 2026, CMHA requires landlords or agents who have not completed a CMHA landlord training within the prior 24 months to complete Mandatory Landlord Orientation before a HAP contract can be executed for a new tenancy; submission of an RFTA packet triggers the requirement, sessions are booked via Eventbrite, and existing HAP contracts and current HAP payments are not affected.

CMHA requires a copy of the Certificate of Occupancy and/or Rental Registration when an RFTA is submitted for a unit in 17 named suburbs (Bedford, Bedford Heights, Brook Park, Cleveland Heights, Cuyahoga Heights, East Cleveland, Euclid, Fairview Park, Garfield Heights, Lakewood, Lyndhurst, Maple Heights, Parma, Shaker Heights, South Euclid, University Heights, Warrensville Heights), and effective October 1, 2025 verifies City of Cleveland rental registrations through the City's Citizen Access Portal.

Rent increase requests go to RentAdjustment@cmha.net and require: landlord and tenant signatures, a HAP contract at least one year old, a passed annual inspection within 12 months of the effective date, at least 12 months since the last approved adjustment, and a rent-reasonable amount; if approved the effective date is the 1st of the month after 60 days from receipt.

CMHA publishes no expedited, fast-track, pre-inspection or self-certification option for landlords; its inspections page describes only standard initial, annual (within twelve months of the previous inspection, transitioning to biennial) and emergency inspections, with emergency violations to be corrected within 24 hours. Inspection scheduling is 216.478.9680 and inspection help is 216.271.2116.

Housing Quality Standards — not NSPIRE — still govern voucher inspections today: HUD's third extension, Federal Register notice FR-6086-N-12 published September 30, 2025, pushes the NSPIRE compliance date for the Housing Choice Voucher, Project-Based Voucher and Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation programs to February 1, 2027.
facts.source_name: HUD — NSPIRE Official Notices and Proposed Rules

facts.claim: CMHA's landlord-facing contact for outreach is Aleksandra Tyler, Mobility & Landlord Outreach Coordinator, at 216.271.2599 or tylera@cmha.net; the HCVP inspections team is at 216.478.9680 / inspections@cmha.net and HAP payment questions go to 216.271.2495 / financehcvp@cmha.net.

facts.claim: Two different Ohio agencies use the acronym CMHA: the Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority (cmha.net, 8120 Kinsman Road, Cleveland, OH 44104, PHA code OH003) and the Columbus Metropolitan Housing Authority (cmhanet.com, 880 E. 11th Ave., Columbus, OH 43211) — landlord incentive programs and payment standards published by the Columbus agency do not apply in Cuyahoga County.

facts.claim: A third Ohio agency, the Clermont Metropolitan Housing Authority (65 S. Market St., Batavia, OH 45103), also abbreviates to CMHA and publishes a HUD-VASH page stating it serves 40 veteran families with contact LeQuita Potter at 513-977-6829 — figures that circulate in search results as if they were Cleveland's but which describe Clermont County, near Cincinnati.

CMHA's Project-Based Vouchers and Moderate Rehabilitation subsidies attach to specific units in privately-owned rental property, so when an assisted tenant moves out the rental assistance stays with the building for the next eligible occupant — the mechanism by which CMHA subsidy can sit inside an EDEN-owned supportive housing building.

The one documented operational intersection between the two organizations is project-based: EDEN's PSH building The Lotus, 1417 E 45th Street, Cleveland, OH 44103 (St. Clair-Superior, 1–2 bedroom, with available supportive services), appears on CMHA's Project-Based Voucher & Mod/Rehab property list with EDEN's own contact details (216-961-9690, edeninfo@EDENcle.org).

The Cuyahoga County Office of Homeless Services serves as the collaborative applicant on behalf of the Cleveland/Cuyahoga County Continuum of Care and as the HMIS Lead Agency — meaning OHS, not CMHA and not EDEN, convenes the CoC and submits the consolidated application to HUD.
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga County HHS — 2025 CoC Program Competition

facts.claim: EDEN's full legal name is Emerald Development and Economic Network, Inc.; it is a 501(c)(3) nonprofit that filed Articles of Incorporation with the Ohio Secretary of State on December 12, 1990 and became operational after its first board meeting on February 26, 1991.

EDEN traces its origin to summer 1990, when the Cuyahoga County Community Mental Health Board identified people who are severely mentally disabled and indigent as its top priority for services and housing; EDEN began with one residence in 1991.

EDEN operates as a contract agency of the Alcohol, Drug Addiction and Mental Health Services (ADAMHS) Board of Cuyahoga County, which provides EDEN an annual funding allocation; EDEN's letterhead reads "A Contract Agency of the Alcohol, Drug Addiction & Mental Health Services Board of Cuyahoga County."

EDEN is not CMHA: EDEN's own FAQ tells applicants "We also recommend contacting the Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Agency (CHMA) to sign up for their waitlist for vouchers and public housing" — i.e. CMHA is a separate organization with a separate waitlist.
EDEN's service area covers Ashtabula, Cuyahoga, Lake and Lorain Counties — a wider footprint than CMHA, whose Housing Choice Voucher jurisdiction is Cuyahoga County.

EDEN owns and manages 850 units of supportive housing for chronically homeless individuals with an additional 112 units in development, plus over 260 units of scattered-site housing for households with a member who has a mental illness; its stat block lists 14 supportive housing buildings, 343 scattered-site units and 65 independent living properties.

EDEN administers rental assistance for more than 2,200 households per month, focusing mainly on persons with disabilities and those who were homeless, and is the primary provider of the Rapid Re-Housing Program (time-limited rental assistance) in Cuyahoga County.

In 2025 EDEN served 4,083 households (7,349 individuals) with supportive housing, rental assistance and housing location/stabilization services; another 1,076 households (2,410 individuals) received homeless/eviction prevention assistance; 1,151 people received emergency shelter at the Norma Herr Women's Center — 10,913 people assisted in total.
EDEN owns the Norma Herr Women's Center, the primary emergency shelter in Cuyahoga County for single adult women.
EDEN's Executive Director is Elaine Gimmel, and EDEN will mark 35 years of operation in 2026.

EDEN is reported to manage approximately 370 Housing Choice Vouchers and zero public housing units, serving roughly 240 low-income households — but this figure comes from a third-party aggregator of HUD data, not from EDEN or HUD directly, and should be re-verified before publication.

A landlord cannot send a prospective tenant to EDEN to "get a voucher": EDEN states "Most of EDEN's programs do not accept applications directly from the public. Instead, the majority of referrals come from approved agencies and systems," and Coordinated Intake/Entry explicitly "do NOT provide referrals for housing, vouchers, or subsidies."

EDEN's housing provider packet lists six voucher program families with the tenant paying 30% of income on long-term supportive housing subsidies (Continuum of Care, H-TBRA), 0% on both Rapid Re-Housing programs (12 months), 30% on Re-Entry and the Housing Assistance Program, and 30–40% on the Mainstream Housing Choice Voucher Program.
