facts.claim: Eligibility for EDEN's supportive housing properties requires federal very low-income status, homeless/chronically homeless/DedicatedPLUS status, and a documented disability, with referrals coming through Coordinated Intake/Entry; tenants pay 30% of gross income.

EDEN's Sponsor-Based Rental Assistance (SRA) is project-based at four partner properties under MOUs — Independence Place (YWCA), Opportunity House (Humility of Mary), St. Joseph's Commons (FrontSteps) and Superior (Famicos) — with on-site supportive services and 30% income-based rent.

EDEN also manages HUD Multi-Family properties under the Section 811 and Section 202/8 programs — Weider-Silver Manor, Franklin Place Apartments and Third Project Apartments — offering 1–3 bedroom units at 30% rent burden for very low-income households with a member who has a documented severe mental illness.

As of July 2026 EDEN's programs page shows the wait lists closed for its Housing Choice Voucher (Mainstream) program, its Housing Assistance Program (HAP), its EDEN-owned scattered-site housing, and its HUD Multi-Family properties (Weider-Silver Manor, Franklin Place Apartments, Third Project Apartments); Eviction Prevention Services are listed as currently unavailable with referrals to NEOrenthelp.org or 211.

To rent to an EDEN client a landlord completes a six-step onboarding: review the Housing Provider Welcome Packet, submit a direct deposit form, submit a Property Owner/Manager Disclosure Form, submit a W-9, list the property with EDEN, and complete the Request for Lease Approval (RFLA) when a tenant is identified.

EDEN's RFLA must be submitted with a completed direct deposit form, a completed W-9, a Certificate of Occupancy or rental registration (required for long-term vouchers), and documentation from the county auditor's website that property taxes are current (or a payback agreement if taxes are owed).

After EDEN receives a complete RFLA, processing takes approximately 3 weeks for long-term voucher programs (Continuum of Care, Mainstream, HAP, all Re-Entry, H-TBRA) and approximately 1 week for short-term programs (Rapid Re-Housing, Community Transition Program); rent determination happens within 3–4 business days of receiving the RFLA.

The HAP (Housing Assistance Program) contract is the agreement between EDEN and the property owner — Part A is generated once a move-in date is confirmed and covers the unit, utility responsibilities, total rent and EDEN's rental assistance amount, and Part B details the terms; the property owner must sign and return the HAP contract in order to receive payment. HAP contracts are not applicable to short-term programs.

EDEN's monthly subsidy payment to the landlord arrives between the 1st and 4th of each month; the first payment is sent within 3–4 business days of EDEN receiving the passed inspection report, but no initial payment is made until the signed lease agreement has been received.
Under EDEN programs the tenant pays their portion of the rent directly to the property owner or designated agent — EDEN pays only its subsidy portion.

EDEN units must pass a Housing Quality Standards (HQS) inspection before move-in and annually thereafter; the initial inspection is scheduled/completed within 3–10 business days of EDEN receiving the RFLA, and EDEN states it will "be unable to enter into contract with any unit that fails HQS inspection."

When an EDEN unit fails inspection the owner has 30 days to make repairs (24 hours for life-threatening conditions), rental assistance payments may be withheld during that period, and tenants are not responsible for EDEN's portion of the rent while payments are withheld.

EDEN does not cover parking fees, key deposits or regular pet deposits — the tenant pays these; and housing providers cannot require pet deposits for document-supported assistance animals "since they are not considered pets by law."

For move-out damage under an EDEN tenancy the owner deducts from the security deposit and must forward the tenant documentation and invoices verifying the amount withheld; if damages exceed the deposit the owner contacts EDEN's Property Owner Information Line to see whether additional resources or programs are available.

EDEN's landlord-facing contact points are the Property Owner Information Line at 216-503-6369 and EDENLandlordInfo@EDENcle.org; EDEN's main line is 216-961-9690 and its office is at 7812 Madison Avenue, Cleveland, OH 44102.

EDEN, Inc. — a separate Cleveland nonprofit that administers its own Mainstream and Gateway voucher programs and is NOT CMHA — advertises financial incentives for housing providers via newsletters titled "Financial Incentives for Housing Providers in Northeast Ohio" (November 2024), but publishes no dollar amounts on its owners page; its Housing Provider Hotline is 216-503-6369 and EdenLandlordInfo@EDENcle.org.

EDEN's published Rent Determination Policy (Long-Term Housing Programs, November 2023) states that "As a recipient of HUD funding, EDEN is required to keep approved rent amounts, as much as possible, at or below the established FMR for the jurisdiction" — EDEN prices to HUD Fair Market Rent, not to CMHA's payment standards.
facts.source_name: EDEN Rent Determination Policy, Long-Term Housing Programs (Nov 2023)

facts.claim: EDEN's Rent Determination Policy allows discretion to approve rent amounts up to 120% of the FMR, but states "All of these must be reviewed by management prior to approval."
facts.source_name: EDEN Rent Determination Policy, Long-Term Housing Programs (Nov 2023)

facts.claim: EDEN runs its rent reasonableness analysis through Go Section 8 to verify a requested rent is reasonable against comparable units, and for its Mainstream Housing Choice Voucher program an affordability calculation must also be conducted before a rent amount is approved.
facts.source_name: EDEN Rent Determination Policy, Long-Term Housing Programs (Nov 2023)

facts.claim: EDEN permits property owners only one rent increase per year per tenant housed, requires the request be submitted on EDEN's website at least 60 days in advance of the proposed effective date, and will only permit a rent increase of up to 10% (with discretion to exceed 10% if the owner has skipped increases in prior years).
facts.source_name: EDEN Rent Determination Policy, Long-Term Housing Programs (Nov 2023)

facts.claim: EDEN's Rent Determination Policy applies to its long-term rental assistance programs and explicitly "does not apply to EDEN's Rapid Re-Housing Program."
facts.source_name: EDEN Rent Determination Policy, Long-Term Housing Programs (Nov 2023)

facts.claim: Shelter Plus Care is no longer a standalone HUD program: HUD's final rule "Removal of Regulations for the Shelter Plus Care and the Supportive Housing Programs" (published April 20, 2026, effective May 20, 2026) removed 24 CFR parts 582 and 583 because those programs "were consolidated into and replaced by the Continuum of Care (CoC) Program following the enactment of the Homeless Emergency Assistance and Rapid Transition to Housing Act of 2009 (HEARTH Act)."

As of the July 1, 2026 edition of the Code of Federal Regulations, Title 24 contains Part 578 (Continuum of Care Program) but no Part 582 (Shelter Plus Care) or Part 583 (Supportive Housing Program), confirming Shelter Plus Care no longer exists as a regulated HUD program.

HUD-VASH combines HUD's Housing Choice Voucher rental assistance for homeless veterans with case management and clinical services provided by the Department of Veterans Affairs at VA medical centers (VAMCs), community-based outpatient clinics (CBOCs), VA contractors, or other VA-designated entities.

Unlike a regular Housing Choice Voucher, a HUD-VASH voucher is not obtained from a waiting list: HUD's operating requirements state the PHA "will not have the authority to maintain a waiting list or apply local preferences for HUD–VASH vouchers" — the partnering VA medical facility refers every eligible veteran directly to the PHA.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH Vouchers chapter (July 2021), §4.2

facts.claim: When a PHA receives a HUD-VASH referral from the VA, it may screen the family only for three things — income eligibility, citizenship, and whether a household member is subject to a lifetime registration requirement under a state sex offender registration program; HUD states "a PHA may not screen for criminal background other than to determine if a household member is subject to a lifetime registration requirement."
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.1

facts.claim: HUD-VASH applicants cannot be denied for past debts or past criminal activity, and cannot later be terminated for them: HUD states a participant "may not be terminated after admission for a circumstance or activity that occurred before admission but could not be considered at the time of admission," and PHAs "cannot deny admission to an otherwise eligible HUD-VASH family that previously participated in the PHA's HCV or public housing program that left owing money or otherwise not in good standing."
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §§4.1, 4.7

facts.claim: HUD-VASH vouchers must have an initial search term of at least 120 days — double the standard voucher; HUD's 2024 operating requirements state 24 CFR 982.303(a), which sets a 60-day minimum, "shall not apply, since the initial term must be at least 120 days."
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.C

facts.claim: CMHA goes beyond the federal minimum on search time: its HCV Administrative Plan states as PHA Policy that "All VASH vouchers will have an initial term of 180 calendar days" — 180 days for a Cuyahoga County veteran to find a unit, versus the 120-day federal floor and 60 days on a standard voucher.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 Housing Choice Voucher Program Administrative Plan, §19-III.E

facts.claim: HUD-VASH initial leases may run shorter than 12 months — HUD waived both section 8(o)(7)(A) of the U.S. Housing Act of 1937 and 24 CFR 982.309(a)(2)(ii) "to allow a term less than one year, without regard to the PHA independently determining that a shorter term would improve housing opportunities." (The waiver does not apply to project-based vouchers.)
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.D

facts.claim: Case management is a condition of the voucher, not an optional service: veterans "must agree to participate in case management in order to receive a HUD-VASH voucher," and assistance must be terminated if the family refuses without good cause, as verified by the VA medical center.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.5

facts.claim: A VA determination that case management is no longer needed is NOT grounds for ending the veteran's rental assistance — HUD states such a determination "is not grounds for termination of HCV assistance," so a landlord's subsidy does not stop when the veteran graduates from case management.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.5

facts.claim: Since the August 2024 operating requirements, HUD-VASH income eligibility reaches low-income households: "PHAs must serve all income eligible veterans, including low-income veterans (up to 80% AMI) in HUD-VASH," and the usual HCV income-targeting requirements do not apply to HUD-VASH families.
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.B

facts.claim: VA service-connected disability benefits are excluded when determining whether a veteran is income-eligible for HUD-VASH, but are added back in when calculating what the tenant pays: HUD states they "are excluded for purposes of determining income eligibility but included for purposes of calculating the total tenant payment (TTP), housing assistance payment, and family share."
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.B

facts.claim: A zero-income veteran cannot be turned away from HUD-VASH: "PHAs may not deny admission to a family with zero income," and the PHA must accept a non-notarized self-certification of zero income at admission and reexamination, verifying income in HUD's EIV system within 120 days after admission.
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.B

facts.claim: For HUD-VASH admissions, PHAs must accept a family's self-certification that total assets are at or below $50,000 (adjusted annually for inflation) with no present ownership interest in real property, and PHAs "must not enforce the asset limitation for HUD-VASH families at reexamination."
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.B

facts.claim: The single biggest landlord-facing difference in HUD-VASH is rent ceiling: HUD waives 24 CFR 982.503(a)(2), (b) and (c) so a PHA may set a separate HUD-VASH exception payment standard of "up to, but no higher than 120 percent of the published metropolitan area-wide Fair Market Rents (FMRs) or Small Area FMRs" — higher than the 90–110% band that applies to regular vouchers.
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.O

facts.claim: HUD explicitly justifies the HUD-VASH exception payment standard by landlord reluctance, noting markets with high HUD-VASH need are "very competitive with a shortage of affordable rental units" and that "landlords may be reluctant to rent to individuals experiencing homelessness due to poor credit history or lack of recent rental history."
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.O

facts.claim: CMHA's HCV Administrative Plan reproduces the HUD-VASH exception payment standard authority verbatim — "PHAs may go up to but no higher than 120 percent of the published area-wide fair market rent (FMR) or small area fair market rent (SAFMR) specifically for VASH families" — but the plan contains no "PHA Policy" statement adopting one, so whether CMHA has actually enacted a VASH-specific standard must be confirmed with CMHA before it is advertised.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 HCV Administrative Plan, §19-III.E and Ch. 16 (Exception Payment Standards)

facts.claim: CMHA administers 611 tenant-based HUD-VASH vouchers and 25 project-based HUD-VASH vouchers according to the narrative of its FY2025 PHA Plan; the same plan's Operations and Management Table instead lists "636 – VASH" plus "25 VASH PBV" (611 + 25 = 636), so the two figures should be reconciled with CMHA before publication.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 Annual PHA Plan (PHA Code OH003, FY beginning 01/2025), pp. 19 and narrative

facts.claim: HUD-VASH is a small slice of CMHA's voucher portfolio: the FY2025 PHA Plan lists 15,974 baseline Section 8 voucher units against 636 VASH and 25 VASH PBV, alongside 170 Mainstream, 400 Non-Elderly Disabled and 102 Family Unification Program vouchers.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 Annual PHA Plan, Operations and Management Table, p. 19

facts.claim: CMHA has selected two new project-based HUD-VASH developments to enter Agreements to enter into a HAP Contract as of October 2024: "Cleveland West – 63 VASH PBV" and "Emerald Senior – 25 VASH PBV," both to serve homeless veterans referred by the Veterans Administration; an existing site, West Village, already carries 25 VASH units.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 Annual PHA Plan, Project-Based Voucher section, p. 58

facts.claim: Cleveland West Veterans Housing, at 3311 W. 73rd Street in Cleveland's Stockyards neighborhood, is a $21.6 million, 62-unit one-bedroom development exclusively for veterans — Cuyahoga County's first LIHTC development designed solely for veterans — where all residents will use HUD-VASH vouchers administered by CMHA, with completion targeted for Fall 2026.
facts.source_name: CHN Housing Partners — Breaks Ground on Cleveland West Veterans Housing (Oct 16, 2025)

facts.claim: Cleveland West Veterans Housing will have VA supportive services delivered on site — a full-time VA social worker, two case managers, a part-time occupational therapist, nursing staff, and a VA Medical Mobile Unit — funded by a 9% Low Income Housing Tax Credit from the Ohio Housing Finance Agency with Cuyahoga County, Citizens Bank and the Home Depot Foundation.
facts.source_name: CHN Housing Partners — Cleveland West Veterans Housing groundbreaking release

facts.claim: HUD-VASH units can be pre-inspected to speed lease-up: under the August 2024 operating requirements, if a HUD-VASH family selects a unit that passed inspection without intervening occupancy "within 90 days of the date of the Request for Tenancy Approval," the unit may be approved — CMHA's Administrative Plan still recites the older 45-day window.
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.N

facts.claim: CMHA applies a standard priority preference for "Veterans and veterans' families" on its public housing waiting list, and awards 40 preference points to families referred by the Veterans Administration for both tenant-based VASH and project-based VASH sites.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 Annual PHA Plan, preferences sections

facts.claim: CMHA commits to screening a VA referral quickly — its Administrative Plan states that after the VA medical center provides a complete referral, "the PHA will perform an eligibility screening within five business days of receipt of a VAMC referral."

CMHA's Administrative Plan confirms the PHA has no role in judging whether a veteran qualifies: "The PHA has no role in determining or verifying the veteran's eligibility under VA screening criteria, including determining the veteran's homelessness status," and the PHA must accept referrals from the partnering VAMC.

A HUD-VASH veteran is defined as someone whose length of service meets statutory requirements, who served in the active military, naval or air service, "was discharged or released under conditions other than dishonorable, and is eligible for VA health care" — a broader standard than an honorable-discharge-only test.

PHAs must accept the DD-214 or the VA-verified Application for Health Benefits (Form 10-10EZ) as verification of Social Security number and date of birth for a HUD-VASH veteran and cannot require a Social Security card; a VA identification card is acceptable government-issued photo ID.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.1

facts.claim: HUD-VASH families may lease units on the grounds of a VA medical center — HUD waived 24 CFR 982.352(a)(5) and 983.53(a)(2), which otherwise prohibit vouchers in units on the physical grounds of a medical or similar institution.
facts.source_name: CMHA FY2025 HCV Administrative Plan, §19-III.E (Ineligible Housing)

facts.claim: HUD-VASH portability is conditioned on continued case management: if case management is still required, families "will be limited to moving to jurisdictions where VAMC or CBOC case management services are available," and the initial VA facility must be consulted before any move.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.6

facts.claim: PHAs may charge a lower minimum rent — including $0 — specifically for HUD-VASH families "regardless of the minimum rent policies established in their administrative plan for other HCV families," because HUD-VASH veterans often qualify for hardship exemptions.
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §II.Q

facts.claim: HUD's most recent HUD-VASH allocation round made approximately $34 million available, which HUD anticipated "will support approximately 3,400 new HUD-VASH vouchers," under Notice PIH 2025-21 issued July 16, 2025.
facts.source_name: HUD Notice PIH 2025-21 — 2025 Registration of Interest for HUD-VASH Vouchers (issued July 16, 2025)

facts.claim: HUD-VASH vouchers are allocated non-competitively to PHAs based on geographic need and administrative performance, and since 2017 PHAs self-identify interest through an annual Registration of Interest notice, requiring "the support of a partnering VA facility" and meeting utilization thresholds.
As of March 2026 more than 95,000 formerly homeless veterans were living in homes leased through HUD-VASH, and the program has housed nearly 250,000 veterans since 2008.
facts.source_name: VA Homeless Programs — What is HUD-VASH?

facts.claim: HUD has awarded over 116,000 HUD-VASH vouchers (including Tribal HUD-VASH rental subsidies) since 2008, and the program operates in all 50 states plus Guam, Puerto Rico and the U.S. Virgin Islands.
HUD-VASH vouchers are excluded from the SEMAP leasing indicator, meaning a PHA's HUD-VASH lease-up rate does not affect its scored performance on that measure.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.11

facts.claim: In a HUD-VASH family break-up the voucher normally stays with the veteran, overriding contrary PHA policy — except where a family member is protected as a victim of domestic violence, dating violence, stalking or sexual assault and the veteran is the perpetrator, in which case the victim must continue to be assisted.
facts.source_name: HUD HCV Program Guidebook — HUD-VASH chapter (July 2021), §4.8

facts.claim: Up to 20 percent of a PHA's HUD-VASH vouchers may be project-based, and HUD's operating requirements include a "PBV zero-HAP option" letting a PHA admit a family whose total tenant payment equals or exceeds the gross rent where the project is on the grounds of a VA facility or provides HUD-VASH supportive services on site.
facts.source_name: HUD — Revised Implementation of the HUD-VASH Program, Docket FR-6476-N-01, §§II.B, II.K

facts.claim: Where a HUD-VASH exception payment standard applies to a project-based development, there can only be one contract rent for similar units — HUD states a "HUD-VASH exception payment standard may only be applied in cases where the project consists only of units made available exclusively for HUD-VASH."
facts.source_name: HUD — HUD-VASH Operating Requirements FAQs for PHAs and VAMCs

facts.claim: HUD-VASH now allows public housing agencies themselves to act as Designated Service Providers, handling intake and "temporary case management prior to referral to the VA," a role created so PHAs can use HCV administrative fees for case management.

The VA's five-step process for a landlord to house a HUD-VASH veteran is: contact the local PHA or VA medical center; review the inspection standards; screen the tenant and complete the Request for Tenancy Approval (RFTA); have the PHA inspect and establish the Housing Assistance Payments contract; then execute the lease, usually for 12 months.
facts.source_name: VA Homeless Programs — Landlords Housing Veterans

facts.claim: Tenant screening remains entirely the landlord's call in VA homeless programs: the VA states plainly that "Screening and approval of Veteran tenants is at your discretion."

The VA tells landlords that with HUD-VASH and SSVF "a portion of the rent is automatically paid on time by a PHA or an SSVF grantee," and that "Accepting vouchers or subsidies doesn't mean getting less money."

Every HUD-VASH and SSVF veteran has an assigned case manager who is also a resource for the landlord: the VA states case managers provide "education about paying rent in a timely manner, abiding by their lease terms, and maintaining a positive relationship with landlords" and "are also a resource for you, the landlord, to help resolve issues with the tenancy."

SSVF grantees can pay a landlord an incentive worth up to two months' rent on top of the security deposit: the VA's landlord flyer states amounts are "equal to up to 2 months' rent, and apply to leases of at least one year," and that "The incentive is yours to keep and does not need to be returned at the end of tenancy."
facts.source_name: VA — Supportive Services for Veteran Families (SSVF) Incentives for Landlords flyer

facts.claim: The SSVF landlord incentive can be "applied once per lease," can be used "to originate, and in some circumstances, renew a lease," and SSVF funding "cannot be combined with other federal funding used for the same purposes."
facts.source_name: VA — SSVF Incentives for Landlords flyer

facts.claim: SSVF is a grant program to community nonprofits, not a voucher: grantees "provide short-term assistance such as security deposits, rental subsidies, housing case management, help with moving expenses, and financial assistance," and many SSVF recipients are co-enrolled in HUD-VASH.
facts.source_name: VA — SSVF Incentives for Landlords flyer

facts.claim: SSVF eligibility is capped at 50% of area median income: 38 CFR 62.2 defines a "very low-income veteran family" as one whose annual income "does not exceed 50 percent of the median income for an area or community," with "extremely low-income" set at 30 percent.

SSVF rent, deposit and moving payments go straight to the landlord, never to the veteran: 38 CFR 62.34 requires that "Rental assistance must be paid by the grantee directly to the third party to whom rent is owed," with parallel direct-payment rules for security deposits, utility deposits and moving costs.
facts.table:
**SSVF temporary financial assistance limits — 38 CFR 62.34**

| Assistance type | Standard limit | Extremely low-income (≤30% AMI) | Paid to |
|---|---|---|---|
| Rental assistance | Max 10 months per 2-year period; no more than 6 months in any 12-month period | 12 months per 2-year period; max 9 months in any 12-month period | Third party (landlord) |
| Utility-fee assistance | Max 10 months per 2-year period; no more than 6 months in any 12 months | 12 months per 2-year period; max 9 months in any 12 months | Utility company |
| Security deposit | Once per 2-year period | Same | Third party (landlord) |
| Utility deposit | Once per 2-year period | Same | Third party |
| Moving costs | Once per 3-year period; storage max 3 months | Same | Third party |
| General housing stability assistance | Up to $1,800 per participant per 2-year period (CPI-U adjusted) — incl. application fees, inspections, background checks, basic household goods | Same | Third party |
| Broker's fee | "Reasonable amount" where a third party identified permanent housing | Same | Third party |
| Emergency housing | 72 hours (single veteran); 60 days (veteran + spouse with dependents); once per 2-year period | Same | Provider |
| Shallow subsidy (62.34(a)(8)) | Up to 50% of reasonable rent, 2-year period without recertification, in counties published annually in the Federal Register | Up to full 2-year period | Third party |

facts.claim: SSVF rent payments are subject to a rent-reasonableness test written into federal regulation: the total rent "must be reasonable in relation to the rents being charged during the same time period for comparable units in the private unassisted market and must not be in excess of rents being charged by the property owner during the same time period for comparable non-luxury unassisted units."

SSVF serves veterans who are homeless or within 90 days of moving into permanent housing: 38 CFR 62.11 covers families residing in permanent housing but at risk of homelessness, those lacking a fixed nighttime residence, and those "scheduled to become a resident of permanent housing within 90 days pending the location or development of housing."

FrontLine Service operates an SSVF program covering Cuyahoga County from 1744 Payne Avenue, Cleveland, OH 44114, reachable at 216-674-6700 (Lake and Ashtabula counties: 440-381-8347), Monday–Friday 8:30 a.m. to 5:00 p.m.
facts.source_name: FrontLine Service — Supportive Services for Veteran Families

facts.claim: Volunteers of America Ohio & Indiana runs an SSVF program from 775 E. 152 Street, Cleveland, Ohio 44110 at (216) 302-2602, requiring that the veteran have a discharge other than dishonorable, income below 50% of area median income, and be in or moving into permanent housing within 90 days.
facts.source_name: Volunteers of America Ohio & Indiana — SSVF

facts.claim: The Louis Stokes Cleveland VA Medical Center — the VA facility that partners with CMHA on HUD-VASH — is at 10701 East Boulevard, Cleveland, OH 44106, phone 216-791-3800 or toll-free 877-838-8262.
facts.source_name: VA Northeast Ohio Healthcare System — Contact Us

facts.claim: Veterans enter HUD-VASH through the VA, not the housing authority: the VA directs homeless or at-risk veterans to "Call the National Call Center for Homeless Veterans at 877-424-3838," free and confidential, 24 hours a day, 7 days a week.

Ohio has no statewide source-of-income protection, so outside a handful of suburbs an Ohio landlord may lawfully decline a voucher: the Ohio Advisory Committee to the U.S. Commission on Civil Rights found that "At present, Ohio does not have statewide source-of-income protections," noting House Bill 182 (2021) remained pending and House Bill 229 (2019) failed in committee.
facts.source_name: U.S. Commission on Civil Rights, Ohio Advisory Committee — Source-of-Income Housing Discrimination in Ohio (October 2024)

facts.claim: The City of Cleveland itself has no source-of-income protection; within Cuyahoga County the municipalities that ban source-of-income discrimination are Cleveland Heights (2021), South Euclid (2015, updated 2018), University Heights (2012, updated 2019), Linndale (2012) and Warrensville Heights (2012), plus Akron, Lorain and Wickliffe elsewhere in Northeast Ohio.
facts.source_name: Signal Cleveland — Eight Northeast Ohio cities ban housing income discrimination

facts.claim: Cleveland's Office of Fair Housing lists its protected classes as "Age, Ancestry, Color, Disability, Ethnic group, Familial status, Gender identity, Marital Status, National origin, Race, Religion, Sex, Sexual orientation" — the list includes neither source of income nor veteran status; the office is reachable at 216-664-2018 or fairhousing@clevelandohio.gov.

Landlords who lease a unit to a Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration client receive a one-time lease-up bonus equal to 50% of one month's rent — the program page states: "Property owners who lease up with a Community Choice Demonstration client will receive a bonus in the amount of 50% of one month's rent."
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Owners (CMHA mobility program)

facts.claim: The Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration gives participating property owners access to a Damage Mitigation Fund of up to $2,000 "in the rare event that a tenant leaves their unit damaged."
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Owners

facts.claim: The lease-up bonus and $2,000 Damage Mitigation Fund are NOT available on every CMHA voucher lease — they apply only when the tenant is a Community Choice Demonstration client and the unit sits in a designated Opportunity Area, which owners must confirm using the program's Address Locator Tool.
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Owners

facts.claim: The Community Choice Demonstration offers "Faster processing" — "Community Choice staff will help you fill out the paperwork and efficiently process it at the Housing Authority" — plus a free listing service through an exclusive unit list; note this is expedited paperwork handling, not an expedited HQS/NSPIRE inspection.
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Owners

facts.claim: The Community Choice Demonstration's "HCVP Financial Incentives for Property Partners" fact sheet lists a Unit Holding Fee under which "1/2 of the security deposit [is] advanced after you take the unit off the market," plus payment of the admin fee and application fee and coverage of "the remaining security deposit amount."
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Property Partner Incentive Fact Sheet (PDF metadata: created January 2023)

facts.claim: Property owners reach the Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration at (216) 770-4048 or info@cuyahogamobility.org, 2800 Euclid Ave., Ste. 401, Cleveland, OH 44115; staff include Michael Lepley (Mobility Program Director) and Leasing Coordinators Lonnie Pratt and Alex Muskal-Hills.
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration — Our Team

facts.claim: The Community Choice Demonstration is still a live CMHA-linked program as of 2026: CMHA's own Winter 2026 landlord newsletter links out to cuyahogamobility.org and to the mobility program application portal, and the demonstration site remains published with 2026 copyright.

The Community Choice Demonstration is a HUD-funded, seven-site randomized controlled trial; HUD published its Rapid Cycle Evaluation in early 2025, an Interim Report is expected in early 2026, and the Final Report is expected by 2028 — so the demonstration is still in its active research window.
facts.source_name: HUD USER — Evaluation of the Community Choice Demonstration (PD60)

facts.claim: CMHA states that with a Project-Based Voucher "the voucher subsidy stays with the building. When the assisted tenant moves out, the rental assistance remains with the building for the next eligible occupant."
CMHA states that "Tenants with PBVs pay 30% of their income for rent and utilities and the rest of their housing costs are paid by CMHA."
CMHA states that "Tenants with PBVs are assisted as long as they live in the unit and continue to qualify for the program."

CMHA operates both programs, stating that "Project-Based Vouchers (PBVs) and Moderate Rehabilitation (Mod/Rehab) are two programs that provide vouchers that are for specific units in a privately-owned rental property."

Under 24 CFR 983.205, a PHA "may enter into a HAP contract with an owner for an initial term of up to 20 years for each contract unit" — versus the typically one-year term of a standard tenant-based voucher HAP contract.

For Project-Based Vouchers, each HAP contract extension "must have a term that does not exceed 20 years," and "[a]t no time may the total remaining term of the HAP contract, with extensions, exceed 40 years."
HUD states that "A PHA can generally project-base up to 20 percent of its authorized voucher units, but under certain circumstances a PHA can project-base additional units."
HUD notes that "Many HCV regulations (24 CFR part 982) also apply to the PBV program," so PBV owners remain subject to the core voucher rules layered under the separate PBV rules at 24 CFR part 983.
CMHA requires that "All owners are required to go on direct deposit to receive HAP payments."
CMHA states that HAP payment dates typically occur on the 3rd and the 16th of each month.
CMHA states that "Rent is abated when the unit fails to pass on two consecutive inspections," and that during abatement "the family is still responsible for their portion of the rent, only."
CMHA states that "If a unit is in abatement for more than 30 days, the HAP Contract may be terminated," and no retroactive payments are issued for the abatement period.
CMHA requires that "There must be a passed annual inspection within 12 months of the rent adjustment effective date" before a rent increase takes effect.
CMHA states that "HCVP does not pay for tenant caused damages" and directs owners to bill the tenant directly or recover from the security deposit.

CMHA warns that "If the owner permits the tenant to move into the unit" before the unit passes inspection and the contract is executed, "the tenant will be responsible for the full amount of the rent."

CMHA requires that all owners who purchase property with existing HCVP tenants complete and submit a Change of Ownership packet, and the new owner begins receiving payments only once that packet is processed.

HUD states that "Aside from serving a special population, Mainstream vouchers are administered using the same rules as other housing choice vouchers," meaning the landlord experience is identical to a standard voucher.

HUD states that "Mainstream vouchers assist non-elderly persons with disabilities" and that "[s]ince 2018, HUD has awarded over $500 million in funding to public housing agencies (PHAs) to support 50,000 new Mainstream vouchers."

HUD states that "Funding and financial reporting for Mainstream vouchers is separate from the regular tenant-based voucher program," which is an accounting distinction at the PHA and does not change the owner's HAP contract.

HUD's Foster Youth to Independence program serves youth "at least 18 years and not more than 24 years of age (have not reached their 25th birthday)" who left or will leave foster care within 180 days, for a maximum of 36 months of assistance.
FYI assistance can be extended beyond the 36-month limit by up to an additional 24 months under the Fostering Stable Housing Opportunities (FSHO) amendments if the youth meets certain requirements.
The FYI initiative operates through PHA "partnership with Public Child Welfare Agencies (PCWAs)," with the child welfare agency required to provide or secure supportive services for the youth.

HUD's Stability Voucher program makes Housing Choice Voucher assistance available to PHAs partnering with local Continuums of Care and/or Victim Service Providers to serve households experiencing or at risk of homelessness, survivors of domestic violence, dating violence, sexual assault, stalking or human trafficking, and qualifying veterans.
HUD's Stability Voucher page lists the program's governing allocation notice, Notice PIH 2022-24, as "(CLOSED)," with the most recent posted FAQ document updated 8/5/2024.

In a March 6, 2025 letter to PHA Executive Directors, HUD stated that after its final Emergency Housing Voucher allocation "the Department will provide no additional EHV CY HAP Renewal funding allocations to PHAs."
facts.source_name: NAHRO — HUD Announces End of Funding Timeline for EHVs

facts.claim: Any remaining Emergency Housing Voucher funds do not expire until September 30, 2035, even though HUD will issue no further EHV renewal allocations.
facts.source_name: NAHRO — HUD Announces End of Funding Timeline for EHVs

facts.claim: HUD-VASH combines HUD's tenant-based Housing Choice Voucher rental assistance for homeless veterans with case management and clinical services provided by the VA at VA medical centers, community-based outreach clinics, VA contractors, or other VA designated entities.

Since 2017 PHAs must self-identify interest in HUD-VASH through an annual registration notice, and HUD and VA use a need formula to invite eligible PHAs — which must have the support of a partnering VA facility — to apply for a specific number of vouchers.

HUD states that "HUD-VASH vouchers are renewed based on actual PHA leasing along with all other housing choice vouchers (HCV)," so from the landlord's side the HAP contract mechanics match a standard tenant-based voucher.
Residents of the Cleveland West Veterans Housing development will use HUD-VASH vouchers administered by the Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority (CMHA).
facts.source_name: CHN Housing Partners — Cleveland West Veterans Housing

facts.claim: In a Continuum of Care Program leasing project the lease is "made between the recipient or subrecipient (the tenant) and the landowner or sublessor," and "[t]he program participant must be the sublessee of the recipient or subrecipient" — so the landlord's tenant is the nonprofit agency, not the household.
facts.source_name: HUD Exchange — CoC Program Leasing and Rental Assistance

facts.claim: In a CoC leasing project "[t]he recipient or subrecipient is responsible for the 100 percent of the rent or sublease rent to the landowner or sublessor, even if the program participant does not pay the occupancy charge in a given month," and "[t]he recipient or subrecipient pays rent on a vacant unit."
facts.source_name: HUD Exchange — CoC Program Leasing and Rental Assistance

facts.claim: For CoC leasing projects, "rent paid on units cannot exceed fair market rent or rent reasonableness, whichever is less."
facts.source_name: HUD Exchange — CoC Program Leasing and Rental Assistance

facts.claim: In a CoC Program rental assistance project a three-party lease is signed by the program participant (the sole tenant), the landowner, and the recipient or subrecipient, and the recipient is not responsible for the participant's portion if the participant misses a rent payment in a given month.
facts.source_name: HUD Exchange — CoC Program Leasing and Rental Assistance

facts.claim: The HEARTH Act of 2009 consolidated three separate McKinney-Vento programs — the Supportive Housing Program, the Shelter Plus Care program, and the Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation SRO program — into the single Continuum of Care (CoC) Program, so "Shelter Plus Care" no longer exists as a standalone funding stream.

The Cuyahoga County Office of Homeless Services is the designated lead agency for the Cleveland/Cuyahoga County Continuum of Care and submits the HUD-mandated consolidated application for federal homeless assistance funds.
facts.source_name: Cuyahoga County HHS — Cleveland/Cuyahoga County Continuum of Care

facts.claim: The Section 8 Moderate Rehabilitation programs — including the Moderate Rehabilitation Single Room Occupancy Program for Homeless Individuals — are governed by 24 CFR Part 882, a separate regulatory part from the tenant-based voucher rules at 24 CFR Part 982.
The Cuyahoga Metropolitan Housing Authority operates under HUD PHA code OH003 and is the public housing authority serving Cleveland and Cuyahoga County, Ohio.

gaps: TIMELINES: No published actual elapsed time from RFTA submission to HAP contract execution or to first check. Everything here is a policy target (15 days to inspect/decide, 10 business days for the tenancy determination, 5 business days to reinspect, 60-day outer limit) plus the payment-run calendar. CMHA publishes no turnaround statistics. Do NOT write 'typically X days' — stack the policy targets, or attribute a range to a named property manager's experience.

WRONG-AGENCY CONTAMINATION — the single biggest trap. The widely circulated '2 business days document review / 10 calendar day inspection / 5-7 business days to HAP next steps / 4 business days to process the contract' timeline, and the '$750 landlord bonus + $750 security deposit' incentive, both come from COLUMBUS Metropolitan Housing Authority (cmhanet.com), a different agency sharing the CMHA acronym. Never use either for Cuyahoga. Same for any Columbus MHA 'common inspection failures' page.
No published turnaround for CMHA's rent-reasonableness determination and rent offer specifically (as distinct from the inspection and tenancy-approval clocks).
Kiosk hours for RFTA drop-off in CMHA's Main Campus lobby are not published. CMHA's general office hours (Mon-Fri 8 a.m.-5 p.m.) are published, but nothing says the kiosks follow them.

The 15-day initial-inspection clock, the 10-business-day tenancy determination, and every other Administrative Plan citation here come from the FY2025 Administrative Plan (effective January 1, 2025). No FY2026 Administrative Plan was found on CMHA's Official Documents page, so these may not reflect 2026 revisions. (One research lane reported the Admin Plan was not locatable at a fetchable URL; the cms3.revize.com copy cited here did return HTTP 200 on re-check 2026-07-21, but it is the FY2025 edition.)
The number of landlords currently participating with CMHA, and the number of units under HAP contract, are not published on cmha.net.

Do not print a named CMHA staff contact. The Landlord Outreach Coordinator's name, direct email and phone appear only in the ~2023 Landlord Participation Guide and could not be confirmed as current staff.
No source found for CMHA's policy on late HAP payment penalties in practice; the Administrative Plan only restates the federal framework at 24 CFR 982.451(a)(5).

DOCUMENT CURRENCY: CMHA's Landlord Participation Guide carries no revision date or version number. It is the most detailed process document but is partly superseded — it describes a 'New Landlord Briefing' rather than the current Mandatory Landlord Orientation, refers to HQS and biennial inspections, and references a March 2021 Cleveland lead-safe rollout. Cite the live cmha.net pages, not the guidebook, for anything procedural. Every guidebook-sourced fact in this pack is marked 'probable' for that reason, and its 'three comparables' and '$50 minimum rent' figures may be outdated.

STALE PAGE: the narrative section of cmha.net/housing/landlords/rent.php is still headed 'Fair Market Rents and Payment Standards Fiscal Year 2025' and references the January 1, 2025 SAFMR implementation, while the Rent Determination tool serves FY2026 standards effective 1/1/2026. Cite the tool for figures and the page only for policy language.

HUD Notice PIH 2024-26 REV-1 still states a compliance date of October 1, 2025 throughout its text because it predates the September 30, 2025 extension. The notice remains in effect but its dates are superseded by Federal Register doc. 2025-19070. Quote its substantive requirements (severity tiers, timeframes) — never its dates.

The inspection checklist CMHA links from its Landlord FAQ is the legacy HUD HQS Inspection Form (OMB Approval No. 2577-0169) with an EXPIRED expiration date of 04/30/2018. It is not an NSPIRE checklist. Treat it as illustrative, not authoritative.

CONFLICT — inspection frequency. CMHA's landlord index page markets 'Biennial inspections'; the Inspections page describes ANNUAL inspections 'within twelve months of the previous inspection' and says CMHA 'is looking to transition to biennial inspections'; the Landlord Participation Guide says biennial (scheduled 60-120 days before the anniversary of the last inspection). The federal minimum (24 CFR 982.405(b)) is 'at least biennially.' Do not promise biennial and do not state any frequency as settled — confirm with CMHA by phone before publishing.

CONFLICT — abatement-to-termination window. CMHA's Landlord Guide says both 'HQS non-compliant for more than 60 days ... may be terminated' (p.16) and 'in abatement for more than 30 days ... may be cancelled' (p.17); the live FAQ says 30 days; the federal rule (24 CFR 982.404(d)(2)(ii)) sets 60 days after the determination of noncompliance. Safest framing: cite the federal 60-day rule and note CMHA separately publishes a 30-day abatement threshold. Do not present a single number as settled.

HQS vs NSPIRE is ambiguous at CMHA. The public Inspections page says HQS is still the standard with a February 1, 2027 NSPIRE deadline, but the FY2025 Administrative Plan contains a full NSPIRE chapter written as current policy. CMHA has not published its own transition date and has not said whether it will adopt NSPIRE early (HUD permits early adoption). Write 'CMHA inspects to HQS today, with NSPIRE arriving no later than Feb 1, 2027' and re-verify before publication — CMHA could switch at any time.

NO ranked 'most common fail items' list exists from any primary source for CMHA or Cuyahoga County. Neither cmha.net nor hud.gov publishes deficiency-frequency data for Cleveland-area HCV inspections. The only CMHA-published list in this pack is the 10-item 24-hour EMERGENCY list — that is a SEVERITY list, not a frequency list, and must never be presented as 'the most common reasons units fail.' A ranked list on a commercial blog (gcrealtyinc.com) and Columbus MHA pages were rejected as sources.

CMHA's re-inspection FEE AMOUNT is not published anywhere on cmha.net. 24 CFR 982.405(h) permits a 'reasonable fee' but the dollar figure is set in CMHA's Administrative Plan. Do not state a dollar amount.

CMHA's third-party inspection vendor is NOT named on any current CMHA page — cmha.net says only 'an outside firm,' and the undated Landlord Guide mentions 'Metro Inspections' in passing. Do not name the vendor in published content.

No CMHA-published statistics on inspection pass rates, average days-to-pass, number of abatements, or HAP contracts terminated for inspection failure. Any 'X% of units fail on first try' style claim is unsourceable.

No source found for what Ohio state law or Cuyahoga County adds on top of HQS/NSPIRE for voucher units. CMHA only advises owners to 'contact the building department of their municipality' for local rental permits or Certificates of Occupancy, noting 'Each municipality has different requirements.' No consolidated Greater Cleveland list exists.

CONFLICT — number of rent-reasonableness comparables. CMHA's live Rent page says TWO comparable unassisted units; CMHA's own Landlord Participation Guide says THREE. Both are CMHA-published, and both facts are marked 'uncertain' here. Either say 'comparable unassisted units in the same market area' generically, or attribute each figure to its specific document. Do not state a single number as fact.

CMHA utility allowance figures were NOT retrieved. The HCVP Utility Allowance Chart is referenced on cmha.net/housing/landlords/rent.php but the dollar amounts could not be obtained. This matters because rent reasonableness and the 40% affordability test are applied to GROSS rent (rent + tenant-paid utilities), so a landlord's advertised rent is not the number CMHA tests. Publish no utility allowance figures.

No static CMHA PDF or chart of FY2026 payment standards exists — the only current source is the interactive tool at apps.cmha.net/rentdetermination, which displays a default table before any ZIP is selected. Link the tool, never a document, and never cite the default table as 'the CMHA payment standard.'

Only a subset of ZIPs was pulled from the CMHA tool (44113, 44118, 44104, 44105, 44127, 44040, 44103, plus min/max/median across the 53 service-area ZIPs). Any other ZIP must be looked up in the tool before publication.

Payment standards for bedroom sizes 5 through 9 are not included in this pack. They are available per-ZIP from the tool (e.g. 44113: 5BR $3,430, 6BR $3,878, 7BR $4,325), but the 8BR column behaves irregularly across ZIPs (several unrelated ZIPs share $2,816), so 8BR/9BR figures must be re-verified before use.

Whether every ZIP in CMHA's 53-ZIP tool list lies wholly within Cuyahoga County was not verified — several (44022 Chagrin Falls, 44017 Berea, 44181) straddle or sit near county lines. Avoid saying 'all Cuyahoga County ZIP codes.'

The ~113% of SAFMR relationship is OUR arithmetic, not a published CMHA figure, and its legal basis is unsourced. CMHA's site says standards are 90%-110% of FMR 'or approved exceptions as applicable,' which the computation contradicts. State it as a derivation if at all, and do NOT assert a reason (exception payment standard authority, Community Choice tiers, or otherwise).

HUD's FY2026 FMR documentation-system pages (huduser.gov 2026summary.odn) returned no populated Cleveland figures. The FMR and SAFMR numbers here come from HUD's published data files, which are primary but are spreadsheets rather than linkable human-readable pages.
CMHA payment standards, HUD FMRs and utility allowances change annually. Never quote a rent figure from memory, and re-verify every rent figure in this pack immediately before publication.

INCENTIVES: CMHA itself advertises NO general landlord signing bonus, vacancy-loss payment, or damage-mitigation fund on cmha.net. The 50%-of-one-month lease-up bonus and the $2,000 Damage Mitigation Fund belong to the Cuyahoga Community Choice Demonstration ONLY, and only for units in designated 'Opportunity Areas.' Never attribute them to CMHA generally. (Both amounts were re-confirmed verbatim on cuyahogamobility.org/property-owners/ on 2026-07-21; note that a separate pass of the program's FAQ page found no incentive amounts, and the program's 'Property Partner Incentive Fact Sheet' PDF is an image-only scan at a 2023 upload path — so no dated document corroborates them, and a live marketing page can change without notice.)

The Community Choice Demonstration publishes no end date, funding cap, unit cap, per-owner limit, tier definitions, per-tier payment standard values, or list of qualifying Opportunity Area ZIP codes / census tracts — eligibility is checked only through an interactive Address Locator Tool. A claim like 'your suburb qualifies for the bonus' cannot be substantiated for any specific address. No list of CMHA Opportunity Area boundaries or exception payment standards could be sourced.

Whether CMHA makes discretionary VACANCY PAYMENTS under the tenant-based HCV program is not established. The Administrative Plan discusses vacancy payments only in the Project-Based Voucher chapter (Ch. 17, 24 CFR 983.352). Do not tell landlords CMHA pays for vacancy.
The EHV owner incentive/retention payment is permissive ('may') with no published amount. Do not quote a dollar figure for it.

CMHA's homepage impact counters ('55,000 people served,' '$8,666,666 distributed to local landlords monthly,' '4,586 units converted to RAD') carry no date, methodology or as-of period. Treat as CMHA marketing, not audited figures — the fact is marked 'probable' for that reason.

SOURCE OF INCOME: the current text of the Cleveland Heights, University Heights, South Euclid, Warrensville Heights and Linndale ordinances could NOT be read directly — American Legal Publishing (codelibrary.amlegal.com) returns HTTP 403 to automated fetches. The suburb list rests on PRRAC's March 2026 compilation and the USCCR reports, and all such facts are marked 'probable.' Verify live ordinance text before making any legal claim about mandatory voucher acceptance in a specific suburb.

Cleveland Codified Ordinances Chapter 665 (Fair Housing) § 665.03 was never read verbatim (amlegal 403). The conclusion that Cleveland has no source-of-income protected class rests on the City's own Fair Housing page (re-confirmed 2026-07-21: 13 classes listed, none income-related), PRRAC, Signal Cleveland and USCCR — four independent sources agreeing on an ABSENCE. Before this goes into landlord-facing legal copy, have someone open codelibrary.amlegal.com/codes/cleveland/latest/cleveland_oh/0-0-0-35272 in a browser and confirm.

Whether Linndale's (Ch. 515) and Warrensville Heights' (Ch. 113) ordinances actually reach housing vouchers is unresolved: PRRAC lists both as source-of-income jurisdictions, but Signal Cleveland says only Cleveland Heights, South Euclid and University Heights specifically include voucher protections. Do not assert that all five Cuyahoga suburbs mandate voucher acceptance.

University Heights is unresolved on two fronts: (a) the 2026 council amendment that would have stripped voucher language from its fair-housing ordinance — search snippets say it was tabled with no new hearing date, but hoodline.com and citizenportal.ai both returned HTTP 403, so the vote and date are unconfirmed; (b) the lawsuit challenging the ordinance — Crain's (Oct 31, 2025) is paywalled, so the case name, court, parties and legal theory are unknown. Do NOT write that University Heights protections are secure, or that they have been struck down. Both would be unsupported.

Whether the University Heights challenge invokes R.C. 5321.19 / HB 430 preemption, the Ohio Home Rule Amendment, or something else could not be sourced. This is the single most consequential open legal question for Cuyahoga County suburb SOI ordinances.

Whether any source-of-income ordinance has been introduced in Cleveland City Council during 2025 or 2026 is unknown. The last documented push was a 2023 statement by the Director of Building & Housing that SOI protections were a Bibb administration priority; no legislative file was found since, and Cleveland's Legistar could not be searched by keyword.
Ohio HB 135's 'stalled' status reflects a March 5, 2025 committee referral with no further action found. Re-check the bill status page before publishing — legislative status changes without notice.

The two voucher lease-up statistics in this pack measure DIFFERENT things and must not be conflated, averaged, or presented as one number: USCCR/CMHA testimony says ~50% of admitted households found housing before their voucher expired; Signal Cleveland says only ~40% find housing within six months. Attribute each to its source and its metric.

Ohio's 'no statutory cap on security deposits' is an absence-of-provision reading of R.C. 5321.16, not an affirmative statutory statement. No government source (Ohio AG, Ohio State Bar) stating it explicitly was found. It is marked 'uncertain' — get a lawyer's sign-off before it appears in copy.

Cleveland Housing Court's eviction FAQ is an April 2021 revision. Its timelines were re-verified verbatim against the document on 2026-07-21, but whether they reflect 2026 practice is unknown, and at least one practitioner source claims real-world Cleveland delays of 90-120 days. Critically, the same answer that gives the 'less than five weeks' figure says cases involving 'tenants on whose behalf the landlord receives a rent subsidy may take longer' — NEVER present five weeks as the timeline for evicting a voucher tenant.

R.C. 1923.05 shows an effective date of April 9, 2025, meaning the eviction-summons statute changed recently (the text mentions a prohibition on naming minor tenants as defendants alongside parents/guardians). The amending bill and full scope of that change were not identified, and R.C. 1923.05 is deliberately not represented in this pack.
Cleveland's tenant Right to Counsel program and its practical effect on eviction timelines for landlords was not researched.

Cleveland Residents First: the ordinance number and effective date are unsourced. A search snippet gave 'Ordinance No. 1039-2023, effective February 6, 2024' but neither the City's Residents First page nor the Signal Cleveland article states it — do not assert it. Registration fee amounts, inspection cadence, and Certificate of Rental Occupancy renewal cadence / 2026 deadlines are also unsourced; the City page documents only the May 30, 2024 fee deadline and the September 1, 2024 certificate deadline.

NO Cleveland-specific rental days-on-market / days-to-lease figure exists in any reachable source. RentCafe/Yardi's Rental Competitiveness Index ranks 139 markets and Cleveland is NOT among them; Zillow Rental Manager and Apartments.com return HTTP 403 to automated fetches. Do not write a Cleveland days-on-market number. The only legitimate substitutes, each of which must be labeled: (a) the 46-day NATIONAL average; (b) IIP Management's advertised 39-day placement guarantee — a marketing promise, not a market average; (c) B2B's '2-4 weeks annually' single-family vacancy estimate — one company's estimate, not a survey.

NO published 'cost of a vacant rental day' figure exists for Cleveland or anywhere. The ~$42 and ~$41/day figures in this pack are OUR arithmetic (rent ÷ 30) applied to verified rent numbers. If used in copy, the derivation must be shown ('at Cleveland's $1,279 two-bedroom Fair Market Rent, that's about $43 a day') and never presented as a cited industry statistic.

Census HVS metro rental vacancy for Cleveland is too noisy to headline: Q1 2026 is 8.1% with a ±4.0-point margin of error, and the 2025 quarters ran 6.0 / 3.3 / 4.4 / 11.7. Any single-quarter Census number will look fabricated next to the private trackers (Yardi 94.5% occupancy ≈ 5.5% vacancy; Colliers 95.8% ≈ 4.2%). Prefer Yardi/Colliers for market-condition claims and reserve Census for 'official government data' framing with the margin of error stated.

Evernest Cleveland does NOT publish its management fee percentage on its own pricing page. The 8%-10% ($199 max) figure comes only from iPropertyManagement's aggregator page, last updated June 7, 2024. Attribute it to the aggregator or omit it.

The iPropertyManagement Cleveland aggregator page (updated 2024-06-07) is demonstrably stale: it lists Realty Trust Services at $99/month with a $159 renewal, while Realty Trust's own live pricing page says $129/month and $199/renewal. Prefer company pages; treat every aggregator figure as ~2 years old.

Monument Real Estate Services (monumentmgt.com) — cited by several search results at 'greater of 10% or $100/month' — is now a PARKED DOMAIN redirecting to router.parklogic.com, and the company appears defunct. Do not cite it or its fees.

Progressive Urban Property Management publishes no lease-renewal fee at all on its pricing page, and High Tech Property Management publishes no base leasing/placement fee (only 25%/50% plan discounts off an undisclosed base). Neither omission may be filled in by inference.

No 2025 or 2026 national property-management fee survey was found. The only industry survey located (iPropertyManagement, 80 metros) was last updated September 7, 2022 — nearly four years stale — and it contradicts itself internally on lease renewals (summary box says $211.92 average flat renewal fee, body says $231.77). That renewal figure is deliberately EXCLUDED from this pack; do not reintroduce it. No NARPM, IREM or Buildium fee survey with Ohio or Cleveland breakouts was found, and no state-level Ohio fee data exists on the sources reached.

No primary or government source (HUD, CMHA, Ohio Revised Code, Cuyahoga County) publishes or regulates property-management or tenant-placement fee levels — these are unregulated private-market prices. Do not imply any government benchmark for fees. Only the rent inputs (HUD FMR) have a primary source.

All market figures in this pack are metro-wide (Cleveland MSA / Cleveland HUD Metro FMR Area = Cuyahoga + Geauga + Lake + Lorain + Medina counties). No Cuyahoga-County-only or City-of-Cleveland-only fee, vacancy, or days-on-market data was found. Do not present metro figures as city or county figures.

Company fee schedules are quoted as published on 2026-07-21 and carry no effective date on the vendor pages themselves — a competitor could change them without notice. Any published comparison must be dated and say 'as published July 2026.'

eCFR (ecfr.gov) blocks standard automated page fetches with a redirect to unblock.federalregister.gov; the CFR text in this pack was retrieved via the eCFR renderer API. The cited human-readable ecfr.gov URLs are correct for a reader in a browser but may fail during machine fact-checking — that is not evidence the citation is wrong.

URL RE-CHECK (2026-07-21): every cmha.net, cms3.revize.com (Admin Plan, Landlord Guide, Portal Guide, HAP calendar, deposit form), apps.cmha.net, cuyahogamobility.org, usccr.gov, prrac.org, codes.ohio.gov, ohiohouse.gov, federalregister.gov, hud.gov, ecfr.gov, huduser.gov and census.gov URL retained here returned HTTP 200. Three clevelandohio.gov URLs were CORRECTED to drop the 'www.' prefix, which does not resolve. The property-management vendor pages, ipropertymanagement.com and rentcafe.com could not be re-fetched from this environment (resolver restriction / 403 bot-block) and were retained on their original lane's evidence — re-open each in a browser before publishing any fee figure.

21 input facts were dropped as duplicates or unsupported and must not be reintroduced from the raw notes: the second copies of the payment-standard-is-not-a-cap, two-comparables, rent-increase, abatement, HQS/NSPIRE-status, 15-day inspection, two-strikes, lead-clearance, portal-72-hour, 15,000-households, Cleveland-no-SOI, Cuyahoga-suburb-list and Cleveland FMR facts (better-sourced versions retained); the unrevised HUD FY26 FMR file (superseded by the revised file, and its as_of was a future date); a guidebook lead-safe fact that collided on id with the City of Cleveland's page; and a CMHA-inspections abatement fact that attributed a Landlord Guide quote to the live Inspections page. One further claim was trimmed: 'NSPIRE will replace the 13 aspects with 3 inspectable areas' is not on CMHA's page and now lives only in the eCFR-sourced NSPIRE fact.

DO NOT publish "113% of SAFMR" as CMHA policy. CMHA publishes no percentage basis; its site says only "90% to 110% of the FMRs or approved exceptions as applicable." The 113% figure is arithmetic across two datasets, no HUD approval document or board resolution was located, and one research pass reports it could not retrieve HUD's FY2026 FMR/SAFMR values directly from huduser.gov (JS-rendered pages) while another cites the .xlsx files — so the cross-check is not independently confirmed. Cite the dollar figures, or describe it as an observed relationship.

DO NOT cite a static CMHA PDF of payment standards by ZIP — none exists. The only publication is the interactive tool at apps.cmha.net/rentdetermination (iframed on cmha.net/housing/landlords/rent.php). The surviving table was scraped ZIP-by-ZIP on 2026-07-21; there is no dated permalink, so re-verify before each use. (Three redundant copies of this table — 0–4 BR and 0–5 BR variants with identical values — were removed as duplicates; the 0–9 BR version is the one to use.)

DO NOT treat CMHA's 5–9 BR payment standards as derivable or verifiable. HUD publishes SAFMRs only for 0–4 BR, CMHA does not publish its extrapolation method, and the 8 BR column does not progress smoothly (ZIP 44141 8 BR = $5,401 vs 9 BR = $4,785; several low-rent ZIPs pinned at $2,816). Report them as published, never as calculated.
DO NOT use the Rent Determination tool's default (no ZIP selected) row as a countywide standard — it is a placeholder identical to ZIPs 44103/44105.

DO NOT claim CMHA's 1/1/2026 payment standards "increased" (or decreased) versus 2025 — there is no year-over-year source, and CMHA's rent page still displays FY2025 narrative text while the live tool returns 1/1/2026 figures.

DO NOT assert CMHA policy mechanics beyond what is on the rent page — exception payment standards, reasonable-accommodation exceptions, the 5–9 BR method. One research pass cites the FY2025 HCV Administrative Plan at a cmha.net Document Center path; another could not resolve a direct PDF URL for it (only the index at cmha.net/about_us/official_documents.php). Confirm the link resolves before citing the Admin Plan, including for the VASH facts sourced to it.

DO NOT assume CMHA maintains separate exception payment standards for reasonable accommodation, HUD-VASH, Emergency Housing Vouchers, or the Community Choice Demonstration — unverified. The mobility demonstration in particular may use different standards in participating ZIPs.

DO NOT state payment-standard or utility-allowance effective dates beyond 1/1/2026. CMHA has historically revised on a January 1 cycle (rates revised September 2025 for the 1/1/2026 effective date) but no announcement of the next revision was found.

DO NOT quote the tenant-share cap without checking wording: sources in this pack render it both as "40% of the family's monthly adjusted income" and "40% of the family's monthly income." The surviving fact uses "monthly adjusted income."

DO NOT treat the utility allowance figures as machine-extracted. The chart is a scanned image with no text layer, read visually at 400 DPI and cross-checked twice. A separate research pass did not retrieve the UA values at all. Verify any single figure against the PDF before using it in a binding document, and note that gross-rent comparisons (e.g. EDEN vs CMHA) depend on these numbers.

DO NOT imply an air-conditioning allowance, or allowances for heat sources beyond natural gas, electric resistance, electric heat pump and the oil rows shown — the chart covers only those and explicitly excludes air conditioning.

DO NOT use this pack for tenant income-eligibility claims. HUD FY2026 income limits for Cuyahoga County / the Cleveland HUD Metro FMR Area were not researched; the only limits here are CMHA's own posted HCV limits effective 4/03/2025.

DO NOT publish an EDEN voucher count. The ~370 figure comes from a third-party aggregator (affordablehousingonline.com) and a 2023 Axios Cleveland article that now returns HTTP 403. Re-verify against HUD's Picture of Subsidized Households or EDEN directly.

DO NOT pick either EDEN scattered-site number without asking EDEN: the /about prose says "over 260 units of scattered-site housing" while the stat block on the same page says "343 Scattered Site Units."
DO NOT use EDEN's 2024 service statistics (4,128 households / 7,378 individuals; 10,211 people) — the /about page is a year stale. Use the 2025 homepage figures and label the year explicitly.
